title: Navy Girl Aerial Demonstration Variant
description: Fighting Magical Girl, Mayflower Shipman, aka Navy Girl has swapped her traditional dress whites for the colors she wears for armed forces day demonstrations. She is accompanied by her Specialized Kinetic Yield, High Altitude Weapon Carrier, or SKYHAWC. (If anyone can find out how to swap a second "K" in there, I'm open to suggestion.)

title: Faculty
description: Guidance counsellor Loki and principle...Virgil. (Yaaayyy! I remember now. )As I recall, their only issue with their devil horned, forked tailed student, is that she was always late to the bell. I really need to read that script again.

title: Blue Demon
description: Everyone I showed the previous incarnation of this picture was in unanimous agreement that I used entirely too much pink. Here's hoping I didn't over correct. ^^'

title: Magical Girl Diva
description: As far as I can tell, the demon girl Diva finally gets her angelic wish. Really I don't care, so long as I get to draw a magical girl transformation.

title: Evil Diva
description: Here is a proposed cover for a long dead series from the independent studio Angry Viking Press. The comic was Evil Diva, and was about a demon girl who wanted to be an angel.  The whole thing took place in a demonic high school, I believe that steeple fingers dude was the principal.
artist_notes: Yes !!  Thanks for noticing.
Yes !!  Thanks for noticing.

title: Princess Nine Commission
description: Artwork from an old drawing of Izumi and Ryo, from the anime, Princess Nine. I still need to find references for Koshien Stadium.I hope this anime gets a reboot. It was great music, and a compelling story, but the animation got cheaper with every episode. This is one series that deserves modern production values.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1810.01
description: Using this year's Inktober  to practice drawing my Streamers characters and machines.Captain Ariel McFly aka Air Force Girl.

title: Anti Tank Girl
description: Army Girl, demonstrates her capabilities as an anti-armor asset, against T.H.R.E.A.T.'s1 fabled Werewolf Brigade during the Battle of Thar.1.) Transformative Hegemony to Rule the Earth for All Time.
artist_notes: Hah! That's perfect!
Hah! That's perfect!

title: 45.35 Kg MBT
description: While the only Streamer incapable of sustained flight, Army Girl can deadlift 50,000 tons, run at mach 0.75, and survive a 15 kiloton detonation, making her an ideal ground operations specialist. With her speed and toughness, she can negate most T.H.R.E.A.T. mobile armor, effectively putting her in the role of a human, main battle tank. Her chief weapons are high explosive P.O.R.T.I.A.* grenades. (Each one can disable a tank. Their ball gowns fragment into shrapnel.) Although she is capable of carrying a great diversity of heavy ordinance, up to, and beyond a 130mm Anti-Infrastructure Rifle. *Poseable Ordinance Resin Ten Inch Action figures. Pictured above.
artist_notes: Well she can punch an opposing tank with sufficient force to detonate its magazine, and pop off its turret like a cork. Actually, she could punch off the turret without having to detonating anything.She can probably cripple any moving vehicle, if striking the right place, like cracking a submarine, or sinking a battleship.There probably isn't much she couldn't wreck.

Yes. Air Force Girl once destroyed a hardened, underground T.H.R.E.A.T. installation, by carrying Army Girl into near earth orbit, before descending into  an improvised dive bombing run, which would see the ground attack specialist used as an invulnerable bunker buster.Army Girl, who thought she was being being carried on a reconnaissance mission, was not amused by this situation.

Still need to think up a drone for her. Don't know what animal to use. Needs to be something that could go well with tank treads.

title: Hench Ride
description: What you see, hurriedly sketched above is a Ka-50 Black Shark attack helicopter. What ride could be more appropriate for the villainous pilots, mutated into hideous shark men, by T.H.R.E.A.T.1, with their heinous C.I.R.C.E.2 weapon?1.) the Transformative Hegemony to Rule the Earth for All Time!!2.) Chromasomal and Intramolecular Re-Combination Emitter.
artist_notes: Nope. All evil.However some are permanent, and some can be cured. I haven't determined the time period, but after a while the C.I.R.C.E. effect is irreversible.

Holy cow. It just occurred to me that there could be good C.I.R.C.E. victims. In fact, since vast segments of the population who are transformed, may end up being permanently altered, even after being liberated of T.H.R.E.A.T. mind control, they may be forced to live out their lives, post war, as intelligent animal/human hybrids.I think I may have stumbled upon a backdoor origin to my other concepts.

I'll be at Otakon again this year. Hopefully with a full comic.

title: Hench Shark
description: Every hero needs good solid punch fodder, to justify their existence.For Coast Guard Girl, it's the mutated animal soldiers, of the Transformative Hegemony to Rule the Earth for All Time.Like the mutated Ka-50 Shark pilot pictured, the altered soldiers retain their skills and training, but their minds are under the thrall of T.H.R.E.A.T..**I decided to get rid of the second "R".

title: A Girl and Her Drone
description: Coast Guard Girl is assisted in the task of rescuing downed Streamers, and civilians, endangered by the evil forces of T.H.R.R.E.A.T.1 by an artificially intelligent Deep Ocean/Littoral Patrol High-speed INterceptor drone.D.O/L.P.H.IN. can deploy flotation devices, and even serve as a life support pod for deep sea rescue. The drone can also carry a prodigious weapons loadout of torpedoes and depth charges for undersea combat.With its advanced AI, D.O/L.P.H.IN. can also offer tactical advice2 to is operator, which Coast Guard Girl uniformly ignores.1.)Transformative Hegemony, Regimented to Rule the Earth for All Time. 2.) Said advice is offered in a cute, high pitched voice.
artist_notes:
Finally, someone gets the homage. Also, since every branch of the service gets one, there is a Marine Corps Girl.
        She doesn't have a drone, though. Just a 100mm Howitzer, modified to be carried as a rifle.Her mission isn't rescue.

Finally, someone gets the homage. Also, since every branch of the service gets one, there is a Marine Corps Girl.
        She doesn't have a drone, though. Just a 100mm Howitzer, modified to be carried as a rifle.Her mission isn't rescue.
No, just a robot. It's still aquatic.
No, just a robot. It's still aquatic.

title: The Fifth Streamer
description: After many requests, I finally decided to add Coast Guard Girl to the ranks of the Streamers.I'm actually liking how this color scheme came out. The U.S. Coast Guard has some cool looking stuff.
artist_notes: She is backed up by a Deep Ocean/Littoral Patrol High-speed INterceptor drone, that can deploy flotation devices.
She is backed up by a Deep Ocean/Littoral Patrol High-speed INterceptor drone, that can deploy flotation devices.

title: Swim War 02
description: Yet more antebellum fan art for Fred Perry's Gold Digger.The apocalyptic level water gun battle wages on. Now risen to even more ridiculous levels by Ace.I wanted to ramp this up to a climactic satellite strike, but at that point even my own prodigious suspension of disbelief began to show signs of wear.
artist_notes: I like the way you think.
I like the way you think.
That was the ultimate attack at the time I came up with this.Now I'm considering aqua-satellite strikes, and water mushroom clouds.H20 Bomb?
That was the ultimate attack at the time I came up with this.Now I'm considering aqua-satellite strikes, and water mushroom clouds.H20 Bomb?
Yea I know. Sometimes I just can't help myself.
Yea I know. Sometimes I just can't help myself.
Thank you.
Thank you.

title: Swim War 01
description: More antebellum fan art for Fred Perry's Gold Digger.I'm sorry that I can't find everything to this sequence. Basically a squirt gun battle is raging between the Diggers sisters, and Brianna  has chosen to escalate conflict in her own signature style.

title: Skippy Sketches  02
description: Some more sketches, or rather a protean page of pencils, featuring my favorite character in Fred Perry's  voluminous Gold Digger pantheon, Skippy Von Richthofen. Keen eyed viewers will notice that I posted an earlier version of the final panel, posted elsewhere in my gallery, under the title Missing Missiles.In this version, I added the vectored thrust exhaust, as well as the obligatory, anime inspired speed lines. (And yet I still didn't correct that oddly angled nose cone. )Side Note: I used the Su-35 Terminator as Skippy's jet because I've seen it referred to elsewhere as a "tri-wing" due to its unique combination of a forward canard, in addition to a main wing, and a movable tail. I figure this qualifies it as a perfect vehicle for the the descendant of the Red Baron.
artist_notes: When my friends ask me, "What character would you like to draw a comic of?" I usually lie, and through out some super hero or another, because I'm fairly certain they've never heard of my real answer--Skippy!!!

When my friends ask me, "What character would you like to draw a comic of?" I usually lie, and through out some super hero or another, because I'm fairly certain they've never heard of my real answer--Skippy!!!

Before it was revealed that Skippy  could out fly the balance of her Night Flight comrades, she was introduced as the weapons procurement officer. I'm sure she has the best countermeasures mercenary money can buy.

Before it was revealed that Skippy  could out fly the balance of her Night Flight comrades, she was introduced as the weapons procurement officer. I'm sure she has the best countermeasures mercenary money can buy.

title: Skippy Sketches  01
description: Here are some random sketches and a thunbnail I did, featuring my favorite character in Fred Perry's voluminous Gold Digger pantheon, Skippy Von Richthofen.
artist_notes: Tracer?
Tracer?

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.31
description: Coming full circle with my final Inktober entry.The U.S.S. Enterprise, no bloody "A", "B", "C", "D", "E", "F", "G", "H", "I", and ESPECIALLY NOT "J"!!!Have you seen the "J"? It looks all spindly and weird. Yuck!Anyway, enjoy the pic. John out.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.30
description: Upon reflection, I probably should have dubbed the last week "Dominion Week", since all of the vessels I've drawn have been ships of the Jem'Hadar or their allies. Today is no different, as I round things out with a chibi-fied Jem'Hadar Heavy Cruiser.Judging from the crescent like, almost chiropteran pylons, sweeping forth from the vessels's primary hull, I would guess that DS9's production designer's followed Matt Jeffries time honored custom of patterning aggressor vehicles after predatory animals; in this case, some form of giant bat. Unfortunately, I always felt the twin warp nacelles, mounted adjacent to its center superstructure, made this warp 9.6, 2,500 Jem'Hadar troop carrying weapons platform more closely resemble some manner of big giant bunny rabbit.But that's just me.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.28
description: Think I'll ratchet up the villainous threat level with today's Inktober chibi.At an astonishing 4265 feet in length, the massive Dreadnaught Carrier is easily the largest, and most powerful of the conventional warships deployed by the major powers during the Dominion War.It's essentially akin to an Imperial Class Star Destroyer, from the Star Wars universe, only with Star Trek weapons;so it can level a planet by itself, and its Jem'Hadar troops don't have to hire out for disintegrations .

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.27
description: Today's chibi villain, the beetle-like fighter that is the mainstay of the Jeh'Hadar space forces.It's just called a fighter. You'd figure they'd have gone with a unique and descriptive moniker, like Scarab or something. I guess the plain name is to reflect the lack of imagination among the genetically engineered troopers.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.24
description: One more week left in Inktober, and my chibi-Star Trek vessel series, so I think I'll shift my attention now to bad guy ships.First on the docket, the distinctive fork tailed, Egyptian ankh inspired, Cardassian Galore Class Warship.With a comparitively modest top speed of warp eight, and a weapons load markedly inferior to the capital ships of three major Alpha Quadrant powers, the Galores, which made up the bulk of the Cardassian fleet, were frequently deployed in groups of three to make up for their shortcomings, and used primarily to enforce the will of the Cardassian Union upon  less developed civilizations.Basically, it's a school yard bully with a warp drive.

title: Inktober161023
description: No this isn't the NX-Class U.S.S Enterprise, of the 22nd century. (BTW, could someone explain why the 22nd century w-5 ships weren't simply called the Enterprise Class, since she was the lead vessel in the series?)This is an Akira class ship, which would first appear in "Star Trek: First Contact", and would later be seen, spectacularly burning and exploding in the back ground of many a battle scene on Deep Space Nine. (Seriously, the Akira's were the redshirts of the fleet.)I'm amused by the name of this ship class, as it is yet another nod, by the Star Trek production staff to Japanese anime, just like:-The Enterprise-D's sister ship, U.S.S Yamato which was a reference to the fabled Space Battleship Yamato anime, as was the aliens from the obviously named Gamelan Five.-The Ferenghi access code, "kayee yooree dahtayee", which was a reference to the characters Kei and Yuri of the "Dirty Pair" anime.-The Nausican's, which were both low rent Klingon wannabes, and a shout out the the Hayao Miyazaki's "Nausicaa of the Valley Wind".If you hear a name in a TNG era Star Trek episode, that sounds similar to something you've heard in anime, know that it is not a coincidence.
artist_notes: I'd like to play this game, but I fear it would sabotage the progress I've made this month toward reestablishing my drawing habit.
I'd like to play this game, but I fear it would sabotage the progress I've made this month toward reestablishing my drawing habit.
Wow, thanks for the info!!! Now can anyone tell me why ships of Archer's era weren't simply called the Enterprise Class?!
Wow, thanks for the info!!! Now can anyone tell me why ships of Archer's era weren't simply called the Enterprise Class?!
True. Would be nice to have seen them kicking bore butt.

I recall reading that the show runners for Enterprise had never watched The Original Series, and hence lacked the proper fundamental knowledge of its history. One line of dialogue in Balance of Terror pretty much negates the level of technology depicted in Enterprise.SPOCK: "As you may recall from your histories, this conflict was fought, by our standards today, with primitive atomic weapons and in primitive space vessels, which allowed no quarter, no captives. Nor was there even ship-to-ship visual communication. Therefore, no human, Romulan, or ally has ever seen the other."Beam weapons, viewing screens, and tranporters, should not even be in evidence on the NX-01.And I'll stop here before I go down the warm, seductive rabbit hole of Enterprise bashing.

The NX-01's are bad designs, they just have no place in a prequel series.In my opinion a blockier, less sophisticated looking craft, like the Daedalus, you mentioned, would be far more appropriate for the period depicted.
These are interesting.The design of the Bonaventure here is quite a bit different than how it appears on the animated series.vignette1.wikia.nocookie.net/m…

I considered the animated series designs canon, since some of them would end up in the remastered effects shots, when they updated original series effects for the 40th anniversary.www.ex-astris-scientia.org/sch…www.ex-astris-scientia.org/sch…BTW, the remastering that was done 10 years ago was way better than the "Greedo shot first" Star Wars remastering. They story was never changed, they just added in the ships that were described in dialog on the show, but were unable to film originally, due to production costs.

I am always fascinated when I encounter people who are only now encountering the original series.It's nice to see that new fans, who did not grow up with the show, are still engage by its story telling. It's quite a testament to the quality of its production.

Well I can't fault you if you find the dated props an impediment to a successful suspension of disbelief. While I still find many of the gadgets portrayed in Star Trek well conceived and prophetic of many modern innovations, I grew up looking at them, and have hence developed an invulnerable armor of nostalgia, regarding humbleness of their origins.Personally, I think it best to look at the original Star Trek in comparison to other science fiction programs of the same age, like Lost in Space, or Planet of the Giants. Despite its dated trappings, there is a gravitas to Star Trek that he other, more juvenile programs sorely lacked. I think it was this more mature and dramatic sensibility that unltimately transcends the fact that Doctor McCoy did surgery, using salt shakers as futuristic scalpels. Space Patrol is very interesting. It amuses me how different groups that have no interaction can come up with similar ideas. For instance, I recently learned that the toy company Mego was developing a line of military action figures, based around a fictional anti terrorism strike force, at almost the same time Hasbro was developing its now legendary G.I. Joe reboot.
True. Would be nice to have seen them kicking bore butt.

I considered the animated series designs canon, since some of them would end up in the remastered effects shots, when they updated original series effects for the 40th anniversary.www.ex-astris-scientia.org/sch…www.ex-astris-scientia.org/sch…BTW, the remastering that was done 10 years ago was way better than the "Greedo shot first" Star Wars remastering. They story was never changed, they just added in the ships that were described in dialog on the show, but were unable to film originally, due to production costs.

I am always fascinated when I encounter people who are only now encountering the original series.It's nice to see that new fans, who did not grow up with the show, are still engage by its story telling. It's quite a testament to the quality of its production.

Well I can't fault you if you find the dated props an impediment to a successful suspension of disbelief. While I still find many of the gadgets portrayed in Star Trek well conceived and prophetic of many modern innovations, I grew up looking at them, and have hence developed an invulnerable armor of nostalgia, regarding humbleness of their origins.Personally, I think it best to look at the original Star Trek in comparison to other science fiction programs of the same age, like Lost in Space, or Planet of the Giants. Despite its dated trappings, there is a gravitas to Star Trek that he other, more juvenile programs sorely lacked. I think it was this more mature and dramatic sensibility that unltimately transcends the fact that Doctor McCoy did surgery, using salt shakers as futuristic scalpels. Space Patrol is very interesting. It amuses me how different groups that have no interaction can come up with similar ideas. For instance, I recently learned that the toy company Mego was developing a line of military action figures, based around a fictional anti terrorism strike force, at almost the same time Hasbro was developing its now legendary G.I. Joe reboot.

First, have you read the Star Trek/Planet of the Apes crossover, entitled Primate Directive? In my opinion, it's the best of the IDW crossovers to date.Second, you shouldn't have to apologize for doing your research. I have no problem with people who want to get things right.

I did hear about the Aliens  crossover. I heard it would be with TNG, which strikes me as odd, since the darker, more strife ridden DS9 would seem a more appropriate setting.I hope it's good, Not every crossover has lived up to the hype. (Star Trek/X-Men, anyone.)

description: Say it with me now."EXCELSIOR!!!"
artist_notes: That was fast!
That was fast!

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.19
description: Today's Inktober Star Trek vessel is the Peregrine Class Attack Fighter.Although small craft operation is a mainstay in Starfleet officer training, their use as tactical assets was eschewed by the dreadnought oriented organization, which viewed fighter combat as a tactic favored by "developing cultures".This attitude changed when Maquis resistance fighters, many of them, expatriate Starfleet officers, armed Peregrine Class courier ships, and used them in hit and run tactics to hold both Starfleet and the Cardassian Militia capital ships at bay, during the three year post treaty border disputes.After outbreak of war with the Dominion, many of the former resistance members were repatriated into  the fleet, bringing with them a rediscovered appreciation for the long neglected art of orbital combat maneuvering. Starfleet was suddenly in the business of building space fighter business.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.17
description: Today's chibi-fied ship of the line is the USS Defiant, otherwise known as Captain Benjamin Lafayette Sisko's Everlovin' Pimp Hand!Class leader in a new generation of "escort vessels", the Defiant was mostly intended to be a test bed for new weapons technologies, that would find use in the larger and more prestigious Sovereign class of starships. Were it not for the emergence of the Dominion threat, it is possible this potent warship would have spent its operational life as a curiosity, forever moored in a geostationary orbit over Utopia Planitia.Given the effectiveness the Defiant class vessels world demonstrate during the ensuing Dominion conflict, I can't help but wonder why Starfleet  kept building Galaxy's and Sovereigns, when the resources used for each could have yielded ten of the "tough little ships".If I had my druthers, space would be bleached with angry swarms of these little badasses.But that's just me.
artist_notes: That makes a good deal of sense.
That makes a good deal of sense.
Macross, or North American.

A chibi XB-70 would be cute. Macross is known to most Americans in its translated incarnation, Robotech. It is the story of Earthlings reverse engineering a crashed alien warship, to make a badass outer space aircraft carrier to take the fight to the distant aliens, come to collect their lost property.Macross is credited for inspiring the "real robot" trend, that was prevalent in anime in the 1980's. Prior to Macross machines depicted in anime, where primary colored magic machines, with only the most perfunctory suggestions of functionality.In the hands of aerospace engineer-turned-production-designer Shouji Kawamori the vehicles of Macross took on a look of real, working war machines . (They were based in part upon the F-14 Tomcat.)
          
         After Macross, and perhaps even to this day, anime relished in a celebration of technical detail and mechanical verisimilitude, that caught the eye of fans the world over. It's more than old school. It's the damn headmaster.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.16
description: Rather than stumbling upon a theme, I'm just going to declare the next seven days, "Fleet Week", and draw chibified versions of Federation starships, in service during the TNG/DS9/VOY period.First up is the USS Phoenix. This is a ship of the Nebula class, which uses many of the hull assemblies of the fabled Galaxy class, only arranged in such a way as to give the vessel a more compact profile. (The Nebula is to the Galaxy, what the Miranda class is to the Constitution.)Beyond its unique placement of warp nacelles below the saucer section, the most distinguishing feature of Nebula ships are the powerful sensor dishes the carry above and after of their main saucers.Despite their compact profile, Nebula class vessels are extremely potent weapons platforms, as evidenced by Captain Maxwell opening up a can of De''e' pup upon the Cardassian fleet, in the episode, "The Wounded".

description: Alright.A Romulan Kestel-Class high speed, diplomatic shuttle.For realsies this time.
artist_notes: The sequel.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.14
description: So I guess I've inadvertantly made this into "Shuttlecraft Week". Today's Inktober entry is a Surak-type, Federation high speed courier/scout shuttle. I've always had a soft spot for this design, even it it only appeared briefly in Star Trek: The Motion Picture, to facilitate Spock's grand entrance. The idea of a standard shuttle pod, mated with a warp nacelle module to increase it's speed and range, is just plain neat. Plus, it just looks cool.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.13
description: As indicated by his earlier sketches, Matt Jeffries (Star Trek production designer, and reason all service crawlways on Starfleet vessels and installations are referred to as "Jeffries Tubes".) had originally intended the Class F "Galileo" Shuttlecraft to be more aerodynamic. His initial design for the craft featured a teardrop shaped main hull, and prominent gull wings.With the production techniques available at the time of the original series, the Aeroshuttle, with its wealth of curved surfaces, proved too complex to build on a limited television production schedule.The Galileo was given a more television production friendly redesign by Gene Winfield, a designer, working for a subsidiary of Aluminum Model Toys, who funded to the shuttle's construction.Winfield took his design cues, from automobiles, specifically, the 1962 Studebaker Avanti, which is likely why the resultant design bears that "orbital minivan" aesthetic.
artist_notes: And to think I almost forgot them this time.
And to think I almost forgot them this time.
One on each side. Just following Jeffries' designs.
One on each side. Just following Jeffries' designs.
I appreciate it!After hanging with you and Nate at this past Otakon, it really hit me that I need to get back in the habit of hitting the drawing board again.Inktober is a godsend foe that.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.12
description: A mercifully simple to draw Class F, "Galileo" Shuttlecraft, deploys its recessed landing skids, and makes it's final approach, just in time for today's Inktober entry.As with the the D-7 Battlecruiser, the construction for the effects models for the Class F Shuttles was funded by Aliminum Model Toys, in exchange for the merchandising rights.Lucrative product placement was yet another technology predicted by Star Trek.
artist_notes: They're really hard to spot, due to the angles at which the full size model was often shot, as well as the resolution of the television sets the original series was usually viewed on.I didn't notice them myself, (Especially the recessed nacelle struts.) until I looked at the remastered edition of the show.sta.sh/01cro76q9v42
In the picture with Kirk and Spock, the two of them are about to carry out a bunch of scantily clad hippy chicks. NO ONE who saw that scene was looking fr the landing struts.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.10
description: Today's chibi starship is the Klingon Vor'cha class battlecruiser.Combining the traditional manta-ray silhouette of the Klingon battlecruisers of antiquity, with the tell tale crimson glow of Federation style Bussard Collectors on the leading edge of each engine nacelle, the Vor'cha was a showcase of the technical exchange between to two former rivals in the years following the Khitomer peace accords.Vor'cha's would be a common sight in Alpha Quadrant space from the 2360's to well into the twenty-fifth century, serving with distinction in every conflict from the Klingo-Cardassian Conflict to the Dominion War.That said, I still think the nose looks like someone stuck the Millennium Falcon on the front of the ship.
artist_notes: After finally adopting CGI for fleet battle sequences, DS9's production design department comes up with a wealth of new ship designs to pad out the Dominion War era Starfleet. Most of them become the orbiting equivalent of redshirts.sta.sh/0elw9cnul7t

After finally adopting CGI for fleet battle sequences, DS9's production design department comes up with a wealth of new ship designs to pad out the Dominion War era Starfleet. Most of them become the orbiting equivalent of redshirts.sta.sh/0elw9cnul7t

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.09
description: Today's chibi Star Trek starship picture is of a Romulan Kestrel-class shuttle. The Kestrel is the Romulan Empire's answer to Starfleet's Danube Class Runabout, and boasts similar size, crew capacity, and speed. The Kestrel-class does boast many features not available on its Federation counterpart, such as cloaking technology, and far more luxurious accommodations in its VIP variant. In fact, this variant is the preferred mode of interstellar transit for many Romulan officials, such as members of the esteemed Tal Shiar, and of course, the senate*. * In case anyone doesn't get the reference: www.youtube.com/watch?v=StF9jr…
artist_notes: I highly recommend DS9. It is the best overall of the Star Trek spinoffs, and the only one with an overarching storyline throughout.In what is arguably the best episode of the series, Garak, who is basically alien James Bond, aids the Federation by doing the reprehensible act of assassinating a Romulan official, and implicating another government. His act is horrible, but serves a greater good; showcasing the show's moral complexity.

Sisko, by the time depicted here, is a lot more of a commanding presence than he was at the beginning of the series.At the beginning he was calm and diplomatic, perhaps in a attempt to engage viewers, more familiar with Patrick Stewart's calm and urbane portrayal of Picard. Over the years, as war breaks out, we see Sisko  become harder edged and more assertive.In continuity, this is a reaction to the pressures of commanding a fleet at war. (With all of the fleet actions her commands, many fans wonder why he was never made an admiral.) Sisko has to shoulder the burdens of sending thousands of men to their deaths for the cause of galactic freedom, not just the occasional handful of security men.Outside of the continuity, I think the producers just decide to "Let Avery Brooks be Avery Brooks" and let the actor tap into the wellspring of badassery that served him so well in the hard boiled detective shows, "Spencer for Hire" and "A Man Called Hawk", perhaps attracting the viewers of those shows as well.The reason Sisko enlists the services of "Space James Bond" here is because Betazed, a charter Federation world, (And home to TNG's Counselor Troi.) had just fallen to the enemy, signalling that the UFP was loosing the conflict.Motivated by this event, Sisko put his most amoral man on the task of securing a new ally, not realizing the depths Garak would plunge to achieve his objective.

Your mother sounds incredibly cool. I should point out that every Star Trek series and spinoff is available to stream on Netflix. You would have to bother with DVD's to watch everything.Also, in the case of both TNG and DS9, both shows really found their footing in their third seasons. You've seen the worst both shows had to offer, so your reticence to continue viewing does not surprise me, but I recommend you do.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.07
description: "From heck's heart, I poke at thee. For hate's sake, I hock my last loogie at theee!"USS Reliant, from The Wrath of Khan.
artist_notes: Yea, those "monster maroons", as the actors called them, were crisp as hell.I can't help but wonder if they were in any way inspired by the landing party jackets from "The Cage"/"the Menagerie". The "Cage" jacket was meant to be worn over the duty shirt, featured a wide collar to reveal the department color collar the aforementioned duty shirt, and had a double breasted fold over Velcro enclosure.These are features the older jackets would share with the "Wrath of Khan" uniforms.sta.sh/01zg99mz2tj2Too many similarities to be a coincidence, in my opinion. Besides, it would be cool if the looked back to the older design.

What would be cool is if Star Trek: Discovery, which is set around the Pike era, would have the blueish grey jackets, and cut them with the sleek lines and dress shoulders of the monster maroons.This would spark an instant visual connection between these eras.Won't ever happen, but it would be nice.
Really, I was thinking of them just using the maroon jakets, only in the Pike era blue gray.I can't help bet think that's what they would have gone with if they had more time and money.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.03
description: Today's Inktober contribution is a super deformed Klingon D-7 Battlecruiser. A little trivia about the D-7; Star Trek's producers had never intended to build a filming model of a Klingon ship. They had bluffed their way out of showing one for two seasons by having the vessel always be out of visual range, or appear only as a sensor blip. Budget reductions for season three seemed to preclude the design and construction of a new alien vessel. Fortunately, Aluminum Model Toys, who had made millions with their licensed models of the USS Enterprise, and the Galilio Shuttlecraft model kits, stepped in and funded the construction themselves, in exchange for another lucrative model license.  Network execs seemed to be oblivious, but the veteran toy company was keenly aware of Star Trek's potential as a merchandising opportunity. I haven't decided if I'm going to continue the theme of drawing SD Star Trek vessels for the entire month. Through my own purchasing of the many successors to AMT's legacy, I have enough references to pull it off, although by Halloween I'll probably be hauling out some obscure ships.
artist_notes: Easily.
Easily.
"Retro" You mean it looked like something from golden age science fiction like Flash Gordon?Very interesting. The Klingons themselves are very much a throwback to such villains as Ming the Merciless.
"Retro" You mean it looked like something from golden age science fiction like Flash Gordon?Very interesting. The Klingons themselves are very much a throwback to such villains as Ming the Merciless.

Yes. The old models didn't have the intricate texture, or "tech-sture" ("Trek-sture"? ) of the later, motion picture/ TNG era, ones.AMT may have been footing the bill, but they still weren't exactly ILM.

title: Inktober: Stardate 1610.02
description: Created in 2009 by artist Jake Parker, the Inktober Initiative  is a challenge intended to promote and develop positive drawing habits in artists, by tasking them to complete one drawing every day during the month of October.  I did not complete a drawing yesterday, to my eternal shame :icononionfail:, but since I've been running my mouth about the initiative, I thought it would be better to start late than never.  I'm starting simple, a quick picture of a super-deformed USS Enterprise-A.  It may interest folks to that the windows near the belly of the ship, which are visible in every "pre-jumping-into-warp" money shot, are view ports for the ships botanical garden. (According to Mr. Scott's Guide to the Enterprise, anyway.)  This section of the vessel is also just aft of the matter/antimatter containment tanks. So in the instance of a magnetic containment failure, the first crewmen to be VAPORIZED will be anyone taking a stroll in the arboretum.  Serves them right.
artist_notes: "And while we will surely log your failure forever in the annals of the internet... this one is still real cool."Uh...thanks?

title: Snow Fall

title: Dragonatomy
description: Pencils for Sparky the Dragon, faithful aerial steed of Princess Adrienne Ashe. There was quite a lot to puzzle out about drawing Sparky. From her powerful upper body, to her incongruous kangaroo legs, to her horse-like snout, and her freaky bat-wings, ol' Sparks was a quite a mythological conundrum. So much so, I opted to work out her bizarre "dragonatomy", on a separate piece of paper, later to be reunited with the Princesses Three through the miracle of Photoshop.  I am forced to admit, drawing helicopters is a breeze by comparison.On a profoundly trivial note, nitpicking keen eyes will notice that I drew English saddles hitched around Sparky's midriff, as opposed to the Western-style saddles, she is usually drawn wearing. I know it's a fantasy kingdom and all, but I just don't see medieval-esque fairy tale knights making use of American saddles.
artist_notes: Flying steeds never have seat belts. From Appa in Avatar: The Last Air Bender, to the flying dog-thing in Never Ending Story, to Aladdin's magic carpet, to the bridge of the USS Enterprise, no fantasy hero ever seems to place any value in passive restraint.

title: Pirate Princeless
description: I'm taking a brief interlude from the wintery mother daughter shenanigans of Evil Diva to create a variant cover for the first issue of the third volume of the highly popular fantasy series, Princeless: The Pirate Princess. The #1 one issue will ship January 28th, and this variant will be a exclusive for  Big Planet Comics.Featured here are the heroine, Adrienne Ashe, the sword wielding woman warrior, who chooses to be a knight rather than waiting for one to rescue her, Bedelia Smith, a freakishly tall (For a dwarf.) dwarven blacksmith whom, after forging Adrienne's unprecedented suit of non midriff showing armor, was inspired to join the young knight's quest, and Raven Xingtao the latest addition to their band.Above is a rudimentary sketch of what will become Adrienne's faithful steed, Sparky the dragon--promise.
artist_notes: I suppose it's hard to see without color, but actually, no part of her body is left exposed by this design. 40.media.tumblr.com/79c85cd44c… She even has a helmet, which I omitted to better show her face.
It's a reinforced  leather sash. Check the diagram. Even if it were a corset, that would still provide coverage and some degree of protection, more so than a bare middy, anyway.

Thanks. I'm glad that there is still someone out there who appreciates the apex of anime as an art form. When I first posted on DA, I got a lot of compliments for being "retro", which prompted me to check out a lot of the new offerings in anime and animation in general, in the hopes of updating my "look". (Of course DVD and the internet make such research far easier than back in the days of shady, back room, 4th generation VHS tapes. )While technically more advanced, I find myself not as inspired by a lot of modern anime, with its never ending glut of emo boys, loli girls, excorcisms, zombies, and infinitely repeating ki-battles , complete with color commentary. "Oh no Obinowashi has just struck Toji with his spiraling fist! No one can withstand that much ki at one time."Worry not, Lilli! Toji will prevail. He has yet to show his TRUE POWER!" I miss the bold decisive characters. I miss the guitar riffs, and I miss the all important robots and mecha that look like they could actually be built.I honestly don't know if I'll find a modern audience, but as I work to get published more and more, I hope to do stories reminiscent of the things that inspired me so many years ago.And I'll bet your Destro look kicks ass.

title: Animal Style Designs 05: Maul Bunny
description: More sketches and floggery to promote Empowered Special #4: Animal Style, which will be in your local comic book store this coming June 5th. (Order it avariciously!!)Since it's Easter, I thought it would be fun to post the Animal Style gang's command-and-control Landmate1, the festively designed Maul Bunny.Essentially, a titanium bathysphere, mounted with powerful hydraulic legs and armored manipulators, the Maul Bunny does not pack the offensive punch of other Animal Style mecha2. It is designed primarily move rapidly around a battle space, observe positions and movements of all combatants, and relay such data to the more heavily armed mecha of its squadron. It carries out this function with a prodigious COTTONTAIL3 sensor array mounted on the aft of its bathysphere module.The COTTONTAIL can track and up to 1,000 separate targets, and lock onto them, allowing every friendly mecha in a battle space to address enemies outside of their individual detection spheres. Although the Maul Bunny features twelve retracting tungsten carbide blades, housed in its manipulator "paws", it possesses no other ranged or offensive weapons. It's the machine's ability to maximize the combat efficiency of entire groups of combat mecha, that makes it stand out--or "hop" out, as the case my be, 1.) Landmate: Big honkin' robot, driven by a human operator, usually seated in the torso. Featured prominently in the manga, Appleseed, by the divine Masamune Shirow.2.)Mecha: Generic term for any internally piloted, anthropomorphic machine--of which I am the master. (Or so it says in the ad copy. )3.) COTTONTAIL: COordination, Target Telemetry and Overview Nodule, for TActical Information and Logistics.
artist_notes: Too bad there's no room for one in her suit.
It just got the reference! King Arthur's knights used it to vanquish the rabbit with the "really pointy teeth!"
Too bad there's no room for one in her suit.

title: Animal Style Designs 04: Zodiac One--Point One
description: More floggery1 for Empowered: Animal Style.This is the first of many, many, many designs for the larcenous Animal Style gang's transport/car carrier/getaway vehicle--Zodiac One. It was quite a challenge, coming up with a vehicle that could perform all there of these tasks. In this initial iteration, I had intended to design something that could carry all of the Hard Suits2 and Land Mates3 internally. Unfortunately, I discovered there would be little room on-panel for the stolen cars, which were sort of the point of the story, with the size of this particular beast, so other options had to be pursued. With this design, I tried in incorporate design elements from the sporty, 1980's Steinwinter truck concept of The Highwayman4 fame. I also tried to give it sort of a croccidile/dragon motif on it's forward section.Since it didn't make into AS, I'll probably use this design somewhere else. Something tells me it will look really spiffy in green.  1.) Floggery: "To advertise and promote a product or service on personal websites or blogs." Term is actually a portmanteau, combining the words Flash: "to display obtrusively and ostentatiously." and Blog: "a Web site that contains an online personal journal with reflections, comments, and often hyperlinks provided by the writer." Still weird to say, "I'm flogging myself." and have that be a good thing.2.) Hard Suit: Roughly man-sized powered armor that augments the strength of the wearer.3.)Landmate: Big Honkin' robot driven by a human operator, usually riding in the torso.4.) The Highwayman 1980's action show, in which a poor man's Mad Max rides the post apocalyptic road ways dispensing justice in the worlds most badass truck. Mad Max meets Knight Rider
artist_notes: Actually, I would use the Landmaster as a basis for the Homey Hauler, the ride of the alternate post apocalyptic version of the Super Homey's. That, and a Striker APC. And I remember the 20mm handgun. Actually, your observation on the foresight on that gun reminds me of an episode of Get Smart, in which Agent 86 and a Kaos agent are meeting for a temporary truce. To demonstrate their good will, the two spies discard their weapons, which are revealed to be machine pistols. At the time, the notion of a concealable, fully automatic weapon was so absurd as to spark a giggle from the laugh track. Twenty years later they were relatively commonplace. Amazing.

title: Animal Style Designs 03: Monkey See, Monkey Doom
description: Another villainous mech design I did for the upcoming Empowered: Animal Style one-shot, due out on June 5th.This is the Brass Monkey, one of two Hardsuits1, fielded by the larcenous Animal Style gang. This simian designed suit is designed for close combat behind enemy lines. As such, it lighter, faster, and far more nimble than the ponderous Terror-Pin, although nowhere near as thoroughly armored.Just for giggles, I decided to pay homage to an old school friend of mine by using a familiar logo as the suit's roundel. 1.)Hard Suit: Roughly man-sized powered armor that augments the strength of the wearer. Term originally used to describe Knight Saber armors, in the seminal anime, Bubblegum Crisis.
artist_notes: Thanks a lot man. I'm glad somebody finally noticed that.
Thanks a lot man. I'm glad somebody finally noticed that.

title: Animal Style Designs 02: Fear the Mecha!
description: Another villainous mecha design for the Empowered: Animal Style one-shot for which I performed the guest artist duties.This mecha is, Terror-Pin a heavy, Landmate1 style mecha which carries a sizable array of Shellfire2 mortars in its rear "shell" assembly. This mecha is designed primarily to serve as artillery back-up to the close-quarter combat Hard Suits3, by showering targets with indirect fire from a stand-off position. (Really, there was no actual need to determine how a giant turtle-shaped robot would be fielded in a combat setting. I just like coming up with that sort of stuff. )When coming up with the line-up for the mechanized car thief gang, Adam wavered momentarily on whether or not to include ol' Terror-Pin, but I campaigned passionately for it, myself being a refugee of the University of Maryland, whose mascot is of course the Terrapin.U of M Representin'! Also, this gives me an opportunity to make a more appropriately fearful version of the aforementioned mascot than was required by my prior commission on the subject.*1.)Landmate: Big Honkin' robot driven by a human operator, usually riding in the torso. Term coined by Masamune Shirow. 2.)Shellfire: Actually an alternate name for Terror-Pin itself, coined by the inimitable Mr. Warren. Much better than my own alternate idea of "Die-Mondback". 3.)Hard Suit: Roughly man-sized powered armor that augments the strength of the wearer. (Think Iron Man.)
artist_notes: If I was doing TMNT, they would definitely have a mecha!!!
If I was doing TMNT, they would definitely have a mecha!!!

Sorry, it can't transform.It's interesting, I've often been asked if jets I've drawn have "Battloid Modes", and now I'm asking if an actual robot has a vehicle mode. I suppose this is why the Transformers franchise continues to endure.

What I meant is that Transformers still has animated series, toy lines and major motion pictures made in its honor. It remains an active and vital franchise, whereas all we Robotech fans have is the 2007 DVD release of The Shadow Chronicles which was well enough animated, but so poorly written and voice acted as to be physically painful to watch. Somebody need to kidnap Andrea Romano, the voice director for Avatar: The Last Air Bender, Batman: The Animated Series, Justice league, Justice League Unimited, Motor City, and Teen Titans. Imagine what she could do with Robotech.

title: Animal Style Designs 01: Paladindrome
description: Thought I'd start posting some of the various and sundry designs and sketches I generated while working on the Empowered: Animal Style one shot, which is due to ship on June 5th. Above is a design for the Purple Paladin's Paladindrome, the sweet sweet ride of a "knight-in-shining-armor" themed emp-verse super-hero. At least I think he's "knight-in-shining-armor" themed, since I don't believe he's been depicted in Empowered canon as yet, but in any case, this gave me the freedom to perform such visual experiments as using the Dark Horse logo as the vehicle's badge.I also thought it would be fun to use the signature crusader knight's cruciform as a theme for the machine, so I decided to give it entirely too many engines, all arranged in a cross configuration--and cross hubcaps. BTW, I strongly believe that every super-hero, everywhere should have some kind of souped-up automobile. (If anything it would add one more marketable accessory to marketers looking to franchise the characters.) I don't care if the hero's own power-set provides him with an alternate means of transit. eventually, everyone loses their powers, even it is that once in a blue moon, temporary instance in which said hero has to prove how tough/brave they are without the advantage of their powers and abilities. Would be nice to have a ride home after the bare-knuckled duel of honor.Think of it...the Emp-mobile...hmmmmm.
artist_notes: And it would probably end up always getting booted, towed away, and impounded.
And it would probably end up always getting booted, towed away, and impounded.

title: Chi-Roines 06
description: Well it's the start of the new year and I, like you, have made an innumerable host of hideously doomed resolutions.Chief among mine are to post more pictures in this long neglected gallery, and to finish my even longer neglected series of chibified fictional heroine drawings. So, kicking off a little rust, here is the sixth picture in the series.Chibi-fied Viper Girl.Initially I was going to make this Sheba, but I totally got the hair wrong, and in my haste, I drew a Galactia bird on her helmet instead of a Pegasus--er--pegasus. So I guess this will be an anonymous female Viper pilot for the present.The composition of a female pilot beside her fighter as the Cylons bear down on her is a shaky homage to a publicity painting  done by Frank Frazetta for the episode, Lost Planet of the Gods. Between you, me and the keyboard, I was always a little bothered by how far the master of fantasy art went off-model with the mecha; but I suppose given my screw-up with the hair, I'm not one to talk.
artist_notes: Thanks, good to hear from you dude.
Thanks, good to hear from you dude.

Of course old style. I still think that stuff's cool.RDM's Galactica was cool starting out, but it really went off the rails. It ended up like Lost, only with spaceships.

At first the reboot was impeccable.The atmosphere really communicated a palpable sense of loss, as humanity came to terms with the end of civilization. They were very good about showing the unthinkable choices and sacrifices that had to be made in a survival situation.They paid attention to critical details. Injuries suffered in one episode kept people out of the action in later stories, things that got broken stayed that way and thought was given to situations and tactics. For a show based on a spaceship, it was very grounded.But over time, the show would be hijacked by plot devices and increasing nonsensical metaphysical hogwash. By the end, it was a sad jumble of deus ex machinas, fan pandering, and pretension. It had utterly lost the verisimilitude that marked its first season, and had become a shadow of its former glory.I credit classic BSG for never pretending it was anything other than a highly entertaining mash-up of Bonanza, Midway and The Ten Commandments--a literal wagon train to the stars.

title: Steampunk Airship
description: My attempt at a steampunk aerial pirate ship for an online comic project, conceived by the feverish imagination of KrazyKrow.I confess that this design was a bit of a challenge for me, as I'm not the biggest fan of Victorian-era aesthetics. (The iconic firearms are sufficiently eye-catching, but I could never warm up to the cat-house filigree that creeps upon every background element like gold inlaid fungus. ) To keep things interesting, I went for a sleeker looking profile, and incorporated a steam-based reaction control system with "Harrier-jet style" thrust vectors. The strip never materialized unfortunately, but upon reflection, I feel it was a good experience, dabbling outside my technological comfort zone.
artist_notes: Hmmm, I keep forgetting about Mucha.In fact I'm overdue to draw a Mucha influenced pin-up. All artist have to do one at some point. [link] [link] [link] [link]I think it's a rule somewhere.

Well airships and Zeppelins do have their appeal. There is something about a huge vessel that hangs in mid air, unbeholden to the influence of gravity.Probably why I like the SHIELD Helicarrier so very much. Now that they have movie's with live action versions of Nick Fury, Iron Man, Thor, Captain America who ride around in said Helicarrier, maybe they'll have a live action version of it as well.

Yes, as the name suggests, it is held aloft by rotor blades, and a power outage would send it into the ground below (Which has happened OFTEN in the comics. In many ways it is the Lt. Worf of fictional vehicles. )But when the power is on and everything is working right, it hangs in the air, cutting a large and imposing silhouette--like a Zeppelin. A possible design for the one to appear in this summers Thor movie. Now I have to see it.

Well I've never heard a remotely positive review for the movie before. This is a new experience.This reminds me of my experience with the Speed Racer movie. It was an unqualified flop at the box office, and most reviewers are united in their vituperation, but I alone enjoyed it.The whole father son dynamic between Pops, Rex and Speed reminded me of the relationship between my brothers, my father and myself. It's no surprise to be that two siblings wrote the screenplay--it all just rang very true. But I seem to be the only one to see this merit in the film.
Are you talking about that computer animated travesty?Wretched.

title: Terp Valk
description: This is my end of a holiday gift exchange with my good friend, Phil Moyer.Normally, as he is also a photoshop aficionado, Phil likes to color other artists' line art himself, but as his end of the exchange was the complete DVD set of the saintly, perfect Galaxy Rangers animated series , I felt obliged to put in an extra effort.I'm hoping you recognize the type of jet in the picture. (Heck with it, if you don't recognize the type of jet in the picture, you're just not worth talking to. ) The character is actually Phil's OC,Aerianne Conner, who is both a bad ass fighter pilot, and a "Highlander" style immortal. (I'm thinking she's going to win that duel with the Kurgan, packin' this sort of heat. )I'm probably doing all manner of injustice to the character in my description, best you visit Phil's site for more pertinent information. Oh, the markings and roundels for the craft belong to the Terrapin Corps, an elite squad of intergalactic, heroic bad asses, of which Aerianne is a member in good standing. Did I mention that my friend is a graduate of the University of Maryland?  I should probably mention, this wasn't the final version of the character as accepted by my friend. He wanted Aerianne's hair to be straighter, and her bangs to be shorter, so the final drawing differs slightly from what you see here. However, I liked this version so much I just felt it shouldn't go to waste. Aerrianne Conner & Terrapin Corps are © Philip J. Moyer
artist_notes: I only have two, one of which is a Transformers Jetfire, that I bought at a Kaybee Toys, way back in 1987. (There were a whole shelf of them that they were clearing out for half off!! Why oh why did I buy only the one? )I lost the armor for the Jetfire sometime in the intervening near quarter century. I still keep the plane on my light table. That was one well built toy.

a.) That's the back of the helmet, my friend.  b.) When in robot mode this particular fighter uses a a weapon like Galvatron used in the animated Transformers movie. In this case, it certainly isn't standard equipment. (You'll have to ask Phil about that particular modification.)

Actually, Phil sent me a more complete answer to relay to you: -------------------------------------------To InsolentWelp:a) Actually, it IS a standard UN Spacy Helmet -- we're just looking at it from the back.  (I was confused at first as well, seeing this draft. The finished version is clearer in that regard.)b) That isn't a chain gun. It's a fusion cannon. (3 guesses who it's from, first two don't count). (For what it's worth, Aerianne is well-trained in the use of the GU-11 gunpod. It's just, as a commander and squadron leader, she has the option to choose more specialized armaments.)c) The Terrapin Corps is a cross-dimensional peacekeeping force, having adventures across the multiverse, trying to keep people from screwing up their homeworlds too badly, putting the smack down on people who deserve it, while cruising around in their giant 4-kilometer-long terrapin-shaped flagship, the USS Testudo.(Whaddya want? I came up with the concept back in 1994...)---- Philip Moyer-----------------------------------------Hope that answers everything.

title: Winged Pony Car
description: The only thing more astounding than the discovery of the miracle fossil fuel, BOA®1 beneath the surface of Mars2 was the subsequent discovery that polymers made from it, repelled gravity when exposed to electrical current.Though practical antigravity, or Antiweight® will have been in use for three decades by the time of America Jones' "NTDDCF" 3, it is only now ever so slowly making its way into the hands of an awaiting public.To date, FT's, or Floating Transports have only been used by heavily trained and licensed services. Such as Heavy FT's, or "HeFT's" for construction and freight hauling; and Light FT's or the "LiFT's" that have become infamous through their use by the DMV.The Medium FT's, or "MeFT's" are potentially the most diverse breed of these vehicles, as they could represent the realizations of science fiction's long welshed-upon promise of "flying automobiles", but they are the last to be fully developed due to the vast multitude of safety concerns they raise.Even so, for years automotive manufacturers have fought legal battles to enable them to build more than the occasional tow truck, or playthings for the astoundingly wealthy.After decades of war with various levels of legislature, as well as the insurance industry, it looks like the first practical, affordable MeFT will finally find its way into the open arms of the common driver.That vehicle is the Pegasus 4.One more headache for the Meter Maids.1.) Blood Of Ares. Legal wranglings with the Wells estate, prevented the use of the name "Cavarite".2.) As every schoolchild knows today, the discovery of BOA® was made in 1976 by the Viking lander, and withheld from public knowledge, until regular travel was established in 20453.) "Not-to-Distant-Dystopian-Cyberpunk-Future." Gotta come up with a more convenient anagram of acronym for that. 4.) Yes, it's painfully obvious homage to the Mustang the first high-performance sports car, truly affordable to the common driver--or "Pony car" as it were.
artist_notes: You made my day, friend. I always figured, that ever since the jet age first kicked off, automobile manufacturers had made tail lights to mock jet exhaust, why not return the favor with a flying car's exhaust? But it was really one of those things I did for my own self edification--I totally did not expect anyone to notice it.

title: Repeat
description: Quick sketch of another character from the "not-too-distant-dystopian-cyberpunk-future. As you can tell from the uniform--another Meter Maid.Miriam Bradley is an inexperienced Parking Enforcement Officer, still growing out her hair from her strict academy bowl-cut. She idolizes America Jones almost like one would a popular upperclassman, following the seasoned veteran around like a lost puppy, and obsessing over her arrest record like the stats of a popular athlete. The younger officer aspires to one day to have an comparable citation record, instill as much respect and fear among the various Commuter Tribes, and have a similarly curvy figure as her more experienced counterpart. (At her present rate of development, only two of her three goals are most likely possible. )Miriam has acquired the nickname "Repeat" due to her preference of carrying two PTD's-- one on each hip.Repeat tends to be over eager, and often barrages offenders, frequently even coating them with tickets. (I am actually considering ginving her two semi-automatic PTD's, whose larger magazines hold 1,000 tickets each. ) At present she is a far cry from AJs cool, "one shot per customer" demeanor, but due her habit of wallpapering her opponents, she is well on her way to being feared by the Tribes.
artist_notes: Cool. I was going for adorable.
Cool. I was going for adorable.

choosing the hair color was surprisingly difficult (Since in an anime-inspired story, I have all manner of outlandish, biologically impossible options to choose from.), but I finally decided to choose one that reflected her level of experience.

Thanks again.I'm glad people seem to be reacting well to the character, and her design. When I wrote that she envied AJ's figure, I was a little afraid some would object to the idea of using "body image" as a character motivation, particularly in the context of a female character desiring a body more in line with the "male fantasy ideal."I suppose I'm just thinking about this too much.

title: PTD: Parking Ticket Dispenser
description: A Samurai has his Katana. A Jedi has his Light Sabre. In the "not to distant dystopian cyberpunk future", Meter Maids will have Parking Ticket Dispensers as their chief instrument of dispensing justice.Since the initials, "PTD", are very similar to Philip K Dick's initials--which are used for the eponymous PKD pistol of Blade Runner fame-- and since DMV is more or less, a cyberpunk story, I took a lot of cues from the iconic weapon. such as the clear polyethylene grips--except that I decided to go with a more "policeman like" palette for their coloring. Initially, I wanted to use the same general proportions from a Colt Army Single Action revolver for this retro-futuristic weapon, but I found that a Colt Anaconda worked better for my purposes. (Many thanks to my good friend and retired postal worker Ken Cox for the gift of the Complete World Encyclopedia of Guns. )A little pseudo-technical information: A standard PTD carries 250 blank parking tickets in the form of compactly folded sheets of interwoven carbon nanotubes. Each ticket is accelerated to approximately 300 meters per second by a series of 110 wafer thin magnetic field accelerators, lining the interior of the dispenser's barrel, and is programmed to unfold within seconds of attaching itself, nigh-irremoveably, to a target. With a PTD, every Parking Enforcement Officer holds the power of life and debt in her hands.
artist_notes: Thanks for the kind words. I don't want to see the day where one can't seriously bring the geek when writing a Comic Book.
Thanks for the kind words. I don't want to see the day where one can't seriously bring the geek when writing a Comic Book.
"Arming and promoting"?My friend, you have no idea.
"Arming and promoting"?My friend, you have no idea.
No, the heroine handles it with one hand, shooting from the hip, like a frontier gun fighter.I'm curious, what about the design suggests to you a two handed weapon?

I've fired guns once or twice myself, and yes I recognize that real world physics really put a crimp in one handed shooting and lookin' all cool, shooting from the hip.I could say that a PTD's linear accelerators has significantly less recoil than that of a traditional gas-operated weapon, allowing single handed accuracy, but, truth be told, while I put a lot of time and research trying to give the guns themselves a sense of verisimilitude; the way they'll be used won't have much to do with recognizable gun etiqette. I guess I've watched too many of this type of film.

title: Tiffany and Charlotte 03
description: More of 's Tiffany and Charlotte, deuling with BMX bikes versus bio-engineered wings.Sorry folks, this is as far as I got.  I figured their adventuring should lead to some manner of trouble, but I hadn't figured out what. What would be the suburban neiborhood equivalent of Draconian Hatchet Fighters?
artist_notes: Thanks.
Thanks.

Your answer makes me realize how the words "Draconian Hatchet Fighter" can be taken out of context. The words do suggest musclebound, axe-wielding thugs, to which Chainsaw Weilding Mainiacs would be an appropriate analog. However, in the Buck Rogers show, a Hatchet Fighter was a sleek, single-occupant spacecraft, optimized for high-speed, ship-to-ship combat. [link] It was the main fighting spacecraft of the Draconian Empire and the tactical equivalent of the Earth Defense Directorate Starfighter. [link]So what would be the equivalent of a Draconian fighter plane? I'm thinking something more along the lines of bullying older kids with their own bikes.Chainsaw Weilding Maniacs are more the purview of Tiffany's mother, or Charlotte's caretaker.

A little trivia. Artist and general design bad ass, Ralph McQuarrie designed the ships for Star Wars, Battlestar Galactica, and Buck Rogers! In fact, the Buck Rogers EDD Starfighter was actually McQuarrie's rejected first draft design for the Colonial Viper. [link] [link]

title: Tiffany and Charlotte 02
description: More on 's insanely adorable cat-girl and bird-girl, Tiffany and Charlotte.As you may or may not recall, I was mystified this two never played Buck Rogers vs. Hawk in their BR inspired, Calvin and Hobbesesque, fantasy, bicycle dogfights. So mystified was I that I scribbled some panels into my sketch book on the matter.Sadly my curiosity was not strong enough to get me to finish this enterprise , but I figured folks wouldn't be offended if I posted what I had.I hope folks can follow what's going on here.
artist_notes: The Warhawk is from Buck Rogers. [link]In the premier of season two, Buck meant the only pilot in the show that was his equal*--a half bird humanoid, named Hawk. The two started out at odds, resulting in THE BEST dogfight of the series. But eventually, the two united against a common foe, and became fast friends. (Never seen that plot before, have we.? ) *Aside from the mysterious unnamed old bad-ass played by Buster Crabbe (Same actor who played the 1930's Buck Rogers ) who saved Buck's bacon in the second half of "Planet of the Slave Girls."--Gawd, I loved that show.
I hate when they do that. Reminds me of how the Boomerang network will only show the same two episodes of Teen Titans.

title: Tiffany and Charlotte 01
description: An old sketch of a pair of characters from one of my favorite comics, Gold Digger, by .For the uninitiated, the duo depicted here are Tiffany and Charlotte, a cute li'l were-cheetah girl, and a genetically engineered bird-girl with the body of a woman, and the mind of a pre-teen, respectively.What is of interest to me is the fact that Tiffany, on occasion, pretends to be Buck Rogers*, while riding around on her bike in Bill Wattersonesque fantasies, yet even though she has a "bestest friend" with her own working pair of wings, she has yet to replay the second season opening dogfight between Buck and Hawk.This omission is a continuing source of mystery to me.  * I mean of course, the 1979 Glen Larson produced, Erin Gray in spandex, Gary Coleman as president, incarnation of Buck Rogers. Which as you all know, is the only one that mattered.
artist_notes: I gave Charlotte, the bird girl, a feminine version of Hawk's armor. [link]Looks better on her in my opinion.

Probably with good reason.I'm sorry to say, after the first two episodes with the Buck/Hawk showdown, they got rid of the "James Bond in Space" angle, put the characters on a ship called the "Searcher", and tried to ape off of Star Trek.There were still some good episodes, like one where Buck was accused of starting the world war that took Earth 500 years to recover from. There was some Hawk badassery in that one, as he helped Buck bust out of EDD custody to clear his name.There was also an infamous episode in which telekinetic space dwarves came aboard the ship, and used their powers to try to "Off-Think" Erin Gray's uniform. (I $#!+ you not! )But for the most part season two just didn't have the crazy, campy energy of season one. Oh, and Mel Blanc stopped providing the voice for Twiki.

I believe the brought Mel Back toward the end, but it was probably "too little, too late."I wonder why they changed the show's format from espionage adventures with all the spandex clad, pretty girls your eyes could stand, all in perils that could only be resolved by either a laser beam gun battle, or an outer space dogfight.That's televison GOLD right there. Why would anyone change that?
Noticed that too did'ja. Why was the only black dwarf, the one takin' orders? I mean all the other dwarves were generals!

title: Maid and Machine
description: Another DMV picture.America Jones once again, this time with a slightly altered uniform.Seeing how actual meter maids were day-glo vests at present, I thought I'd try one out on our heroine. The low belt, bike pants were suggested by  so I thought I'd try them for a spin. It's been a while since I designed AJ's uniform, so I thought I might try to freshen up the look.You'll note here  AJ's PTD is in transit between her hand and its holster. I always loved the old Galaxy Ranger cartoon and how the straps on their holsters would pop open on command. I thought it would be cool if the entire holster opened up and somehow "magleved" the PTD to its user.On more note, to facilitate this unhinging action I made a slit running down the side of the holster, similar to the ones the Colonial Warriors used in the original Battlestar Galactica. Yea, I know--I'm putting wayyy too much thought into this.The robot in front of AJ is a Roboot, and semi-autonomous device that attaches itself to the tires of cars with delinquent parking tickets. This little critter is one of two, carried in integrated housing in AJ's Light Floating Transport or LiFT.Though suited to fill the role of spunky sci-fi robot, Roboots are regarded with unqualified dread by the average commuter.
artist_notes: Actually they sing "I'm Never Gonna Let You Go." by Sergio Mendes.

Galaxy Rangers was possibly the best adventure cartoon ever. Certainly the best of the 1980's. It was basically "Firefly: The Animated Series". Zachary Fox was like Mal. Telepathic Niko () was like River, and Doc Hartford was their Book. (Actually, he's more like how I'd imagine Zoe and Wash's son turning out, now that I think of it. And River had more than a little of genetic super-weapon Shane thrown in, but you get the picture. )Of course to me, Rangers corrects what in my mind is Firefly's greatest flaw--lack of bad ass space fighters. Both show even got canceled after one season. Too cool for TV I guess. You really seem hung up on detail. Let me ask you. Are you saying that the sources you are citing give you the impression that fundamental skills like anatomy and perspective are unimportant?You say that they look for detail first. Do you believe that they don't look for the fundamentals at all?

There were two episodes that had animation on par with the opening: Psychocrypt and Sundancer. I'm always disappointed by Sun Dancer, because it was the second half of a two-parter and all the cool violence happened in part one. Part two was about a horse race. An awesomely animated horse race, but still... At least we'll always have Psychocrypt.
You should get the DVD. Psychocrypt is in the volume entitled "Phoenix". I don't think Sundancer has been collected, but I think you can find it on Youtube. [link] [link]

Gotta admit, I love that too. Half the fun of appreciating a good work of art is in learning precisely how the artist arrived at the particular decisions--why'd they use a certain angle? What compositions they tried and discarded, and why? What previous works or unrelated objects influenced their vision.Learning the process whose result is a favored image is oftentimes as enjoyable as the image itself. I can't stand it when artists put ellipses in their artists comments. If I were running things, a deviation would not post until an artist typed a minimum of 25 words in their artist comments. Of course, if one forces the issue, folks'll probably do passive/aggressive junk like typing "One, two, three, four..." and so on. Oh well.

title: Rifle Maid
description: Here's a sketch of America Jones, the "not-too-distant-cyberpunk-dystopian-future's"  toughest Parking Enforcemnet Officer! (AJ, for short. ) The weapon she has slung over her shoulder is a Palomino 61M Assault PTD-- the inevitable rifle version of her trusty sidearm, the PTD (Parking Ticket Dispenser.*)The Assault PTD dispenses the same 5.5'' x 8.5" parking ticket as the pistol type, but at a significantly higher rate of fire. The larger barrel beneath the primary muzzle fires larger 6' x 9' tickets, which can paper an average man to a wall from as much as 250 yards. APTD's are largely used for crowd control situations.*Yes, the PTD is a mild tribute to the eponymous PKD of "Blade Runner" fame. When I realized how the initials came out, I almost felt obligated.
artist_notes: Damn, I can not debate that. *Redraws hips*Take another look, let me know what you think.And let me know what you think of the rifle concept, ya gearhead. What's the use of having a visitor who builds his own robots, if he doesn't comment on the tech?!

You know what? Scrutinize away, my friend. I just did a side-by-side comparison between the modified and unmodified jpegs, and found myself aghast that I allowed myself to post the first version. If I can't take criticism, especially when it's accurate, then what the hell good am I?Also, thanks for the comments on the gun, and sorry for begging for them like some kind of art diva. I've gotten so accustomed to the abundant flow of feedback from other sources  that I've gotten a little spoiled. If I want that kind of dialogue in this gallery I'm going to have to work for it here, and not think I'm in any position to demand it.Thanks again.

I had it right in the freakish original drawing, but as I was making corrections, I had the thought, "Hey wait a minute, men and women open their coats in the opposite direction. Wouldn't that hold true for the direction of their belts?"And all I had to do was look in a magazine. That's the trouble with the things you think you know. You tend to do less research.

As I recall, I was trying to capture that skinny Sonoda-esque look in the figure. Don't know why, just experimenting, and not all experiments succeed.Oh well. That's why we have sketchbooks.Any thoughts on the rifle concept?

Well, since the picture was still at the pencil stage, I decided to fix the problem. The "granny shorts" were beginning to bother me as well. Yea, sorry no "alternate uses" here. Besides, perps are by no means "occasional" here. In fact, there will be need for bigger weapons in the future. The "not-to-distant-dystopic-cyberpunk-future" future.

Hmmm that might work better than "Not-too-distant-dystopian-cyberpunk-future", which doesn't even lend itself to am acronym. (NTDDCF? See what I mean?)Spaceflight, flying cars, light-up umbrella handles, and all of the street-crime and political corruption of home.

Ah man. You have altered my perception of service life.I really bought into the bad ass recuitment advertisements in which Keith David says, in an appropriately bad ass voice, "If someone wrote a book about your life, would anyone want to read it!"Transposed with a whole montage of bad ass dudes jumping out of Blackhawks, and other equally bad ass dudes skipping the surf in a Rigid Hull Inflatable Craft, with their M-4's at the ready.Now you've put the image of some guy like me saying,"Roll to save against mystical damage"In a circle of dudes with pencils, graph paper and twenty-sided dice.If I had known the Navy was more like the latter--I would've enlisted!

That is so cool, but not altogether surprising when I give it more than a quarter second of thought.It reminds me of an episode of "Mail Call" I saw in which I spotted a Cobra symbol spray painted discreetly on the turret of self propelled artillery piece.We geeks are everywhere!

Heh, that was more a drawing flub than a fashion statement. Check the drawing now. I've redrawn the shorts. I suppose she still won't be fashionable, but hopefully she looks a little less freakish now.I am giving some thought to changing her uniform. I've noticed that meter maids nowadays wear day-glo vests--sort of like what deer hunters wear, only yellow as opposed to orange.I was considering giving AJ, and her less tough comrades, a bullet-proof version of such a garment. I suppose, since this is the "not-too-distant-dystopian-cyberpunk-future*", the vests could be self illuminating as well as proof against small arms fire.Don't know what to do about the shorts though. *I really should come up with an acrinym for that. It's becoming increasingly annoying, typing that out. Hmmm. NTDDCF?

I guess I'll just have to copy and paste the phrase a lot. Also, since you can link a garment to a decade, (Something that wouldn't have occurred to me. Like most guys, I use automobile model years to measure time. "Hey that's a Lebaron convertible, this film is set in 1983!") I'm guessing you might have an eye for fashion.Any suggestions for a uniform update?
Got me all curious now. I'll send you a note with my addy.
Well thank your for taking time to give me your input like this. I'm sure it will be very helpful. I have faith in the wonders a fresh point of view.

It's not too hard to recognize cars from subsequent eras. Cars after 1967 got progressively more tank-like, until the fuel crisis could no longer be ignored in the late 1970's, and since that time cars have been looking steadily more like used bars of soap, with a brief flirtation with tank-likeness again during the Clinton years when everyone had money.

Yea, but the biggest, "tankiest" SUV, stills lacks the raw intimidation factor of a 1970's Cadillac sedan.  A Venza looks like its taking a family on vacation. A Fleetwood looks like it has five bullet riddled bodies in the trunk--And probably does. Actually, I learned to drive in such a beast. After that, there is nothing I can not parallel park.

Got me all curious now. I'll send you a note with my addy.
Well thank your for taking time to give me your input like this. I'm sure it will be very helpful. I have faith in the wonders a fresh point of view.

It's not too hard to recognize cars from subsequent eras. Cars after 1967 got progressively more tank-like, until the fuel crisis could no longer be ignored in the late 1970's, and since that time cars have been looking steadily more like used bars of soap, with a brief flirtation with tank-likeness again during the Clinton years when everyone had money.

Yea, but the biggest, "tankiest" SUV, stills lacks the raw intimidation factor of a 1970's Cadillac sedan.  A Venza looks like its taking a family on vacation. A Fleetwood looks like it has five bullet riddled bodies in the trunk--And probably does. Actually, I learned to drive in such a beast. After that, there is nothing I can not parallel park.

I guess I'll just have to copy and paste the phrase a lot. Also, since you can link a garment to a decade, (Something that wouldn't have occurred to me.
