Huh. I know who you're talking about, but I always thought is was Seras? Hmmm, I see you get the same images fro either spelling on google images. It's amusing watching how the "L's" and "R's" get transposed in anime translation. For instance, I heard that if you'd ask Sonoda he'd tell you it was supposed to be Larry Vincent But there are other differences. Seras/Cellas wear a skirt. And AJ has dark skin, and purple hair. (You can't tell that from here, of course. )

Well for now, I just have a few images from my own gallery. [link] [link] , [link] [link]There are also a few strips I drew of the character, but I don't want to overload you. With hope, there will be other venues for you to see my intrepid maid of meters. For now, I hope you enjoy my humble scratches.

Thanks, I'm glad you enjoyed what you saw. With hopes, in the next few weeks and months you'll have more to see.I do take my influences from animations. I worship at the altar of Kenichi Sonoda and Bubblegum Crisis.  Of course, I know that makes me all Old Sküle now, but I like what I like.

('Course now, I can't name the character who carries it, Yoko. ) And I have come up with a vehicle that can mount even heavier PTD's, although I need to come up with a design for the actual mounted weapon. (As well as a name for the 6' x 9' tickets--- "SIXTY-NINES"!!! )A Tackleberry-esque meter maid?  That's a new one. As is a Gatling PTD. I'm thinking the latter is more likely, but who knows?

No, but there's another character who wears them. Actually, she's a meter maid AJ encountered in her past. [link]My working name for the character is "WiNiE", (Woman with No Eyes) which is a pretentious reference to a character from the movie "Cool Hand Luke", in which Paul Newman plays a man who is sentenced to a chain gang for vandalizing parking meters.

"Traffic Warden?"  That actually sounds cooler than "Meter Maid". Perhaps I should have set the story in England. (Then I could name the heroine, "Rita". )I'm afraid you might be a tad disappointed by the NTCCDF's Special Combat Weapons and Armor Deivision, or "SCWAD".Sure, they have a breathtaking assortment of visionary weapons, equipment and vehicles, and bad ass storm troopery armored uniforms in a fashionable klien blue, but whenever they get a call, a meter maid has resolved the matter by the time the arrive on the scene.They are the Maytag™ repairmen of the future.

All the duties once performed by dogs are done by spunky l'il robots in the NTDDCF1. The exposure of animals to harmful situations like explosives detection is considered barbaric by the more evolved denizens of the future. The "K-9's" employed are more like Doctor Who's old robotic sidekick--only, you know, called something else, since I don't have any rights to that name. 1.) Not To Distant Dystopian Cyberpunk Future, in case you were wondering.

title: Bunnies for Hire
description: Now that you've met Daniel Dukke and Ferdinand Fox, let me introduce you to two of their chief villains--The Hench-Bunnies.This halter-topped and miniskirted pair of miscreants expertly employ all matter of weapons to create mayhem and destruction, sometimes for pay, and sometimes for kicks. Skilled enough to survive multiple encounters with both the Dukke of Death and the Teenage Killing Machine, The Hench Bunnies present a unique threat due to the usually deadly Ferdinand's critical awkwardness around members of the fairer sex.  One bat of an eye, and the slayer of 1,000 ninjas is out of the fight. It's tragic really. The Hench Bunnies, as well as Deeds and Ferdy are totally © John H. Staton.

Don't I get any credit for being able to distinguish a M1915 Vickers from an M1910 Maxim? I'll look into halter tops, (Big Chore that.) but how are the both not wearing miniskirts? Their hem lines are well above their knees. What am I missing?You too deserve a sketch for nailing me on this technical flub. Name your character.

A sketch of you eh? That quite a bold thing to propose after all this talk of halter tops and miniskirts. (Or "butt tourniguets", as I occasionally like to call them.)I find myself tempted to use this as an opportunity to correct my previous fashion faux pas.You'd get a machine gun too, of course.

Could you try sending the link for the gun again? When I clicked on it, I got a site, but no picture of any specific firearm came up, and I must confess your description has certainly whetted my interest. When I look at your message here I see an emoticon and some gibberish immediately following the link. I figure that some DA protocol probably confused part of your original link.Very curious to see your other links.

Hee hee hee.Pimped out thought this may be, this is actually a fairly common gun. Or at the very least, a replica of a popular firearm.This is a Taurus PT92, which, despite a few minor details, is pretty much a licensed reproduction of the infamous Beretta Model92. [link]  The Model 92 was Bruce Willis' sidearm in "Die Hard" [link] (Remember the climactic scene where he taped a pistol to his back? That was a '92.) It was also Mel Gibson's sidearm in Lethal Weapon [link] , and has been the standard issue sidearm for soldiers in the United States Army, Airforce, and Marine Corps since 1985, under the designation M9. [link]You sure you wouldn't want something more exotic? Beretta did make a machine-gun version of the Model 92, called the 93R [link] . Looks a little bit like Megatron and "pimping it out" would be  a simple matter of photoshop.Just a thought.

Do you mean "big" as in "physically large firearm", or do you mean "big" as in "widely used"?  Oh well, no big deal, I see here that the overall "pimpage" is the main factor.A drop leg rig is a good suggestion, as it would go well with a nice, shiny butt tourniquet ; I think a shoulder rig would also be good, cause it would show off yer--er--halter top! (Don't worry, I'll do my fashion research this time.)
Let me know when you post it.
Just need to take off the coat. Like the glasses. They make you look all crazy.

Or at the very least, a replica of a popular firearm.This is a Taurus PT92, which, despite a few minor details, is pretty much a licensed reproduction of the infamous Beretta Model92. [link]  The Model 92 was Bruce Willis' sidearm in "Die Hard" [link] (Remember the climactic scene where he taped a pistol to his back? That was a '92.) It was also Mel Gibson's sidearm in Lethal Weapon [link] , and has been the standard issue sidearm for soldiers in the United States Army, Airforce, and Marine Corps since 1985, under the designation M9. [link]You sure you wouldn't want something more exotic? Beretta did make a machine-gun version of the Model 92, called the 93R [link] . Looks a little bit like Megatron and "pimping it out" would be  a simple matter of photoshop.Just a thought.

Do you mean "big" as in "physically large firearm", or do you mean "big" as in "widely used"?  Oh well, no big deal, I see here that the overall "pimpage" is the main factor.A drop leg rig is a good suggestion, as it would go well with a nice, shiny butt tourniquet ; I think a shoulder rig would also be good, cause it would show off yer--er--halter top! (Don't worry, I'll do my fashion research this time.)

title: The Original Dukke of Death
description: Get ready for a trip down memory lane.This positively archeological Berol Prismacolor™ marker illustration depicts my first ever original characters in all their glory.Holding the Desert Eagle, sporting the double-breasted Armani suit, and posing in front of the red Lamboghini Countach, is none other than Daniel Dukke ( Pronounced "duck" . As you can no doubt guess, this character is the inspiration for my bizarre screen name. )Daniel Dukke is an independently wealthy, sophisticated jet-setter who travels the world finding evil where he can, and extinguishing its practitioners in the simultaneously coolest and grisliest ways possible.I never decided whether or not he was a plain-clothes policeman, an undercover detective, a private investigator, a secret agent, or simply an overdressed vigilante, although at one point or another during his existence, he's exhibited signs of being all of the above. In all honesty, I really didn't care about such particulars so long as the criminal body count was sufficiently high. As I look at this picture now, the profound impact of the television program Miami Vice™ upon my impressionable young psyche, becomes readily apparent. As I recall, I even owned one of those ridiculous Don Johnson "Pastel Ice Cream Man" suits back when the show aired. (I hope to God no one ever photographed me in that get up. ) Looming large behind Mr. Dukke, is his equally bad ass1 side-kick, Ferdinand Fox, an aspiring crime-fighter who learned martial arts by watching the films of L. Bruce Preslei2 set on fast forward! Acquiring preternatural fighting skills in this manner, the newly minted Teenage Killing Machine sought out the world's foremost slayer of evil-doers, Mr. Dukke, to learn from him how to become a great adventurer.When he thinks of it, Daniel will occasionally say something wise and "mentory" to Ferdinand, but for the most part, he just points gifted fighter at the inevitable, incoming cloud of blood-thirsty Ninja Cats and lets him go wild. Foxes, as it turns out, make excellent human shields.During my college days, I inflicted the adventures of these characters upon the hapless University of Maryland campus. I probably have a few copies of the collection I made still gathering dust in my attic. Every now and again, I consider having DD & FF take place in the same "nobody looks twice at a five-foot-two-inch mosuegirl" universe as Most Dangerous Bookstore, and having Daniel be a former associate/ex-boyfriend of Cassandra Cat. In this manner I could explain the bookstore cat's uncanny acumen with dispatching ninja-pirate-zombies. 1.) I made up these characters when I was eight, so it's quite a rush to be able to use the phrase "bad ass" without fear of reprisal. 2.)FYI, L. Bruce Preslei was a recurring character in the strip. He was a rock star who acted in a host of mediocre kung-fu/musicals before disappearing mysteriously in 1975. He is also © John Staton, as are Daniel Dukke, and Ferdinand Fox.
artist_notes: Yea there was/is a collection. Heh, I totally wasn't expecting to hear any interest at this point. I was all braced to hear how rickety, antiquated, and 80's everything looks here. The next battery of commets will no doubt be along those lines, so for now its good to hear from someone who remembers.Send me a note, let's see what we can do about getting you a copy or two.

The only thing you could possibly blackmail me with would be pictures of me in my old white "Sonny Crockett" suit, complete with sockless deck shoes.Actually, there are probably a great many things one could blackmail me with, but that one sticks out in my mind.Look forward to your note.

I'm actually quite tickled that you like this antiquated drawing. I don't know why, but I really appreciate it. Thanks.I occasionally think about resurrecting these characters; of course with hilariously superficial changes like giving Daniel a red Ford GT and getting rid of Ferdinand's parachute pants. It's fun to think I might have one or two more stories left in me featuring these two."Human" shield is just a generic term. it would still be Ferdinand charging into the face of all the dangerous, flying objects. Remember Mr. Dukke, the duck, is the smart one.

You make a great point. It IS high time I do something with all this crap I have sparking between my ears.I guess that's why I appreciate DA so much. Putting out concepts before potential readers in this manner helps convince me they won't be blown off as the ravings of a old nut. But still, I do need to generate the nerve to expose my work to a larger forum.On another matter. I was just remembering how much research I had to do to get the right views of a Countach. When I was mentioning moments ago about giving "Deeds" a new GT, I just "google imaged" more views than my last trip to the library yeilded of Lamborghinis! Times they are a changin.'

Oh yea, and the law firm run by Gort from "The Day the Earth Stood Still".That reminds me, did you see that super-crappy remake of that movie?  They had Gort turn into friggin nanomachines! Gort! The inspiration for Scott Summers, Cylon Centurions, and the  Knight Two Thousand. Gort, the imposing silver standard against which all killer robots are judged, reduced to a cloud of stoopid nanomachines. I am so glad that movie flopped. There should be a rule, a LAW in Hollywood that states: If you get to a point in your film making career, where you believe a Giant Silver Robot with Laser Beam Eyes isn't enough to hold an audience's interest on its own merits, that you should be ejected from the film industry on the spot!There just aren't enough slaps to go around.End rant. And thanks for remembering.

Sorry no well-meaning, incompetent aviators here. Being a veteran of the New Zealand conflict, Daniel Dukke is an expert fighter ace among his other copious attributes. When the need arises to get around the globe fast, he pilots his privately owned Lockheed YF-12 interceptor (Imagine an SR-71 with weapons.) himself.For the most part Deeds does his own driving and piloting, in spite of the fact that Ferdinand openly covets the driver's seat of Mr. Dukke's insanely cool sports car. Knowing this, Daniel tends to use this fact as a means of behavior modification."Leave a thug alive for questioning, and you get to drive the car."Sadly, Ferdinand rarely gets to drive the car.

That's amusing, since the drawing and the characters depicted therein are positively ancient. I should send you the old graphic novel I made with them, although I should warn you, while some of the humor still holds up, I was learning how to draw as I produced it.On the other thing, progress is slower than I'd hoped. My friends tell my I'm being too much of a perfectionist and they're probably right. I think I'll start posting the character design drawings and such here in the gallery to whet people's appetites. Or a least let them know I'm alive.How are things going on your end? Talk to Ron or Jason at all?

In the comics, the original red headed Batgirl was shot and paralyzed by the Joker. [link]  (Which should have been the straw that finally made Bats kill the pasty bastard, but that's just my opinion. )Since that time, Babs has taken on the new identity of the handy-capable Oracle [link]  , who provides intelligence and information to the super-hero community, providing a long needed counterpoint to data-hacking villains like The Calculator.Barbara has also trained two young women to succeed her in the mantle of Batgirl, an Asian Girl, Cassandra Cain,  [link]  and most recently, the blonde daughter of the super-villainous Trickster, Stephanie Brown. [link]  In the case of Barbara Gordon, while I miss her as the original Batgirl I actually admire DC Comics' courageous decision not to magically overturn her paraplegia. In her new role as Oracle, and the mentor of her successors, she has become a character who despite injury, is never taken out of the fight. [link]

title: Connie and Kwewsah
description: I don't remember if I said anything about adding Star Trek characters to my long-procrastinated Chiroine's series, but since the new, non-sucky movie came out, I've seen so much Chibi/Cutesy/Sd/Etc... Star Trek art that I felt moved to sketch up some li'l ships. Here is a sketch of a Constitution Class vessel, along the lines of the new movie's design. I couldn't quite make out what the new Klingon vessels looked like during their all too brief appearance, so I decided to go with the "tried-and-true" D-7 cruiser, or to go with the "cutesified" theme "kwewsah" design.I'm sure any and all true Klingons would surely disembowel me for what I've done here.Star Trek, and all related characters and concepts are © and ™ Paramount Studios.
artist_notes: Over that last few weeks, I've seen every other aspect of Star Trek get chibified,[link] so it seemed like a natural progression to me.

title: Missing Missiles
description: More with the dog fight comic. Here I wanted to portray a jet threading its way through one of those ludicrously intense swarms of impossibly maneuverable, random trajectory missiles you see in every anime ever filmed!Seriously, I think I saw one in Peach Girl the other day.
artist_notes: Huh. It's always amazing what you don't see once you get used to looking at a drawing.Good observation.

That's funny, 'cause I drew the speed lines on a separate piece of paper to photoshop in later.I was beginning to lose track of things with all the missiles. (So much so, I forgot to throw in the hidden beer can.) I figured that adding the speed lines to the original pencils would give me vertigo.While there are some details I need to fix on the plane, I'm going to opt out of "squash n' stretching" it in this picture. I may add motion blur to a few elements, like the foreground missiles, but that can wait until I ink and color everything. We'll see.Considering how bad ass the pilot's supposed to be here, I'm reserving the option to actually have her reading a newspaper during all this.

Nope the plane is in fact dodging all the missiles. It's a long established custom that missiles never hit anything in anime, they just swarm about getting ever so close to the heroes craft in order to better demonstrate what a complete and utter bad ass he or she is.I think I've seen those cluster bomby killamajigs you mentioned. It's awesome seeing them all fan out. It'll be interesting to see how they get protrayed in anime.

"Missile Spam?" There's that phrase again.Ah, I see. I didn't realize there was a term for those crazy missile fusillades in anime. Considering they were old hat as far back as 1986, I really should have been surprised if there wasn't  one by now.That's going to save me a lot of typing in the future. However, I really do like the word "fusillade" Thanks for the compliments on the jet. It's still got some tweaking before I hit it all with the inks, but I'm glad the reactions been positive so far.
Hmmm, a Missile Spam Glossary. Thanks, This is SO coming in handy in the future.

Yea, cartoon or movie battle has very little to do with the real thing.For instance, the chief advantage of the aircraft carrier is the ability to strike targets well beyond visual range. In fact, many of the legendary carrier battles of WWII took place without the opposing carriers ever coming within sight of each other. However, in a fictional show, like Battlestar Galactica, battlestars and basestars slug it out within artillery range of one another as each vehicle's fighter compliment mix it up while trying to dodge flack and fratricidal ordinance.Ridiculous, but it looks awesome.

Yea I know. It would be fun once to do a comic in which some anime kid with a Zac Effron haircut stumbles upon a war machine "Unlike any he's ever seen before..." during the requisite alien invasion, and when he takes it out for a spin BÜME! is immediately blown into so many free-floating ionized particles.Actually, maybe he's not disintegrated, let's leave a barely recognizable corpse in the twisted remains of the mech, so some grizzled veteran can survey the aftermath and say."Stupid kid? What he think he was going to do--beat battle hardened veteran without an ounce of training? What he should have done was run!"Then out of nowhere Horatio Cain will show up, don his cool shades and say,"Yea, every one knows how to do that.WIIAAAAAUUUUGGH!We Won't get fooled again...But I digress.

Why yes, yes it is.This is a panel from a Gold Digger annual submission that my lazy ass never finished.It was to feature Skippy Von Richtoven showing off her preternatural flying skills.They idea was that she would be flying the only non-stealth jet in a training exercise against other Night Flight regulars.I figured that, with Skippy's unique heritage, the tri-winged Terminator would be the only appropriate ride for her--painted bright red of course.

Interesting.While not a stealth fighter per se, it does have a reduced radar cross section. Plus, its reverse swept wing would give it extreme maneuverability, and there's also it's resemblance to the X-29 piloted by her beloved rival Kevin Koss.Excellent suggestion. However, the Su-37 just looks cooler and more menacing to my taste, and its more pronounced moveable horizontal tails gives it a much more prominent three-wing look, appropriate for the descendent of the Red Baron.But your idea has much merit.

The one and only.This was a panel from a fan comic that I never got around to finishing. I mainly drew it, because, with its small canard, forward of its main wing, the Su-37 Terminator has been referred to as a triple wing aircraft. It seemed so obvious that the successor to the Von Richtoven family name should  pilot the spiritual successor to the old Fokker Dr-1.In the fan strip, the balance of Night Flight, flying Y--23's, (Think Raptors, only not ugly.) spar against Skippy, writing her off, due to their stealth advantage. She pastes them all of course, her skill overcoming the advantages of numbers and technology enjoyed by her opponents.On the matter of Skippy, I recall reading an issue in which Penny converses with what is apparently Skippy's ghost. I desperately hope she wasn't killed off. I loved that character.

That's certainly a relief to here. BTW, your comments regarding this old picture have inspired me to fish out some of the other Skippy related artwork I had lying about.johnstaton.deviantart.com/art/…johnstaton.deviantart.com/art/…Enjoy.

*Googles "Itano circus"*Wow! Now I finally have a name for this phenomenon. Thank you sir.
Itano? That is a person? (Pardon my ignorance. You could say "Itano" is a non dairy desert topping, and I'd believe you at this point.)Tell me more anything about this individual.

Thank you for the link, and the lesson.I appreciate learning about the people behind the works that I love.Every time greatness is touched upon in any human endeavor, it is because of the participation of certain individuals who possess the right vision, the right grasp of their surrounding circumstances, or the right cocktail of unique talent to take their profession to the next plateau of excellence.Every landmark achievement, whether it be a dynastic sports team, a successful car line, or an influential animated cartoon, have at their core these special people, and their story only enriches one's appreciation of their masterwork.

title: Cockpit Shots
description: Pencilled panels from a submission I am working on. My ambition is to capture the dynamics of a Top Gun style dog fight on a comic page. Hard to tell, I know since you can only see the controls here.One thing that will always mystify me is why the "Bad ass fighter pilot" never became a legitimate movie genre. I mean few things on earth are as inherently cool as fighter jets. And I'm sure the military would cooperate in the filming of any number of motion pictures that made them look like flying rock stars, and yet still no Top Gun II.*You really have to wonder. *OK, there was Iron Eagle II. But that's just not the same.
artist_notes: I dunno mate. I've seen some pretty sweet dog fights in manga. As a matter of fact, manga's pretty much the only place outside of Antarctic Press comic books that I've seen dogfights done competently. But I do appreciate  the vote of confidence.I love WWII dog fights as well. I just wish they had more 3rd and 4rth gen fighter jets in them. (Ever see Final Countdown? [link]  Best of both worlds. )

And True Lies was 15 years ago!! It will forever puzzle me why movies aggrandizing jets are so few and far between.I honestly think Hollywood has no real idea what's actually cool.For instance, this past weekend, I saw the remake of The Day the Earth Stood Still. It was horrible.I could fill the screen with complains about this waste of film. For one thing they had Keanu Reeves play the super intelligent alien  Klaatu, and award winning actor John Hamm as the impotent authority figure who stands in the control room giving pensive, disaster movie exposition. That's like hiring Rembrandt and Jackson Pollack and  asking Pollack to paint your portrait, while Rembrandt paints your fence.The most poignant issue I have with the movie is the unforgivable misuse of Gort. They take one of science fiction's most classic, and visually influential robot's and replace him with a cloud of nanomachines, because obviously some Hollywood type thought that a giant silver robot with a cycloptic laser beam eye wasn't cool enough to hold an audience's attention on its own merit!!When you get to the point in your film making career that you think things like fighter jets and giant laser-eyed robots aren't cool enoght for you, you need to retire on the spot.There endeth the rant.

title: HIND-Butt
description: I honestly don't know if anyone remembers at this late point, but a short while ago I submitted a comic page depicting a Mil Mi-24 Hind-D attack helicopter about to meet what looked like a bad fate. [link]I promised if I ever found the subsequent page, I would post it.Well here it is. Marine Girl headbutting a Hind.In retrospect, I wish I had spent a lot more time on the third panel. I am disappointed with myself that I opted for "flash lines" to depict the destruction, rather than a detailed drawing of the ruined husk of the doomed machine as it spends its final instants aloft before surrendering to the welcoming embrace of gravity.I may have to redraw this on principle.

title: 'Peake Performance
description: The story behind this aging marker drawing is easily as amusing as the image itself.Years ago, I was commissioned to do a drawing for a Star Trek fan club, the eponymous U.S.S. Chesapeake.They wanted, quite simply, for me to draw a ship for them. I didn't realize that the club at the time would have been more than satisfied had I simply put the name "U.S.S. Cheasapeake"  on a drawing of Star Trek: The Next Generation's famed Galaxy Class vessel; so being the mecha-nut I am, I designed an entirely new vessel.I decided to add a third warp nacelle, (Please note that I did this drawing well before the episode "All Good Things..." aired.) integrated into the saucer section, so that it would not become a lumbering, bulky target should the vessel have to separate. Said Nacelle would add power to the weapons system, in particular the dedicated saucer dish particle beam, when the ship's sections are united. Keen eyes will also note quite a preponderance of torpedo tubes lining the ships hull.Anyway, when I presented this battle wagon to the club, they rejected it out of hand because it wasn't the opulent explorer's vessel that Picard rolled around in. Normally, I would have simply gone back to my drawing board, but, being gleefully fluent in treknobabble, I decided it would be fun, this time, to debate the benefits of my ideas. At that point, I addressed the club and defended my concept like defense contractor fighting to keep a plant open.I pointed out that, in Next Generation, Starfleet's rules of engagement staunchly dictated that commander's were never to fire unless fired upon. Given that fact, and the situation that their two greatest territorial rivals: The Romulans and The Klingons boasted a prodigious first strike capability as well as an aggressive fleet disposition; it was certain that Federation starships would be called upon to respond to initial aggression.To carry out such a doctrine under those circumstances, Starfleet would need a second strike capable front line vessel, and quite frankly, the Galaxy Class simply wasn't it.I went on to enumerate the Next Generation Episodes1 in which the Enterprise 1701-D was incapacitated by obsolete, underpowered, sometimes ancient vessels whose only tactical advantage was that of first strike. TheFederation clearly needed a vessel that could absorb an attack from a war-surplus Bird-of-Prey piloted by a Ferenghi skeleton crew and respond in such a decisive manner as to deter future incursions from ambitious would-be aggressors.Just to drive the point home, I also mentioned that of the six total Galaxy-Class vessels, christened at that point in the 'Trek timeline,2 (Which were supposed to defend the Federation from the Romulan Star Empire which boasted at least 12 separate comparable D'Diridex-Class cruisers.) three were lost during deployment. (Four, if you count the Enterprise-D itself, during "Generations". And the Galaxy was just a test-bed ship!)I actually ended up selling the club on the idea of the ship. If I was a fictional Federation defense company, I would have made a mint contracting that gun-laden behemoth to Star Fleet. Well I would've, if there was money in the Star Trek future.1.) Just to assuage the fears of the countless, annoying fuddy-duddies, who feel I'm going way too deep in my analysis of fictional outer space fleet dispositions and tactics--"Yes, I am aware this is all just a T.V. show!" so please feel free to bugger off.2.) Edit: I had previously made what I considered to be a complete list of the names of the Big E's sister vessels, but I had included the USS Melborne, which a keen-eyed observer pointed out was an Excelsior-Class vessel. For the sake of accuracy and brevity, I felt it was better to omit the listing.
artist_notes: I'm almost embarrassed to admit I got  the background by dipping an old toothbrush in water soluble, white Goache paint and flicking the bristles with my thumb over a sheet of black construction paper.The drawing was 11" x 17" lineart done with Sakura Pigma pens, colored with Berol Prisma Color markers, color xeroxed, cut-out and rubber-cemented on to the construction paper. I used a little more Goache to emphasize some of the ship's numerous windows.I have to admit, Once I scanned the picture into the computer, I added a little Photoshop glow, around the Nacelles, and Bussard Collectors. (A cheat, I know, but I couldn't help myself.)

Funny thing that. Back when I did this, it was actually all the rage to dip a toothbrush in ink and use it to create blood spatters. (I believe it was Jae Lee who popularized the technique.)I just reversed things by using gouache on black paper.

Well if the Chesapeake rammed the Scimitar, she'd immediately board the latter!It's actually naval doctrine to board a rammed enemy vessel. What a wasted opportunity.

I love the philosophical tightrope the Federation always dances across.They always say their vessels are ships of peace, yet an average starship alone can lay waste to an entire nuclear-age civilization without breaking a sweat and they alone are called upon to defend the Federation in times of war.I think that the Federation would better served by having a seperate corps of diplomatic vessels, and a fleet of dedicated warships to patrol its borders. But then we couldn't have "West Wing" style political intrigue episodes one week, and "Red October" style adventure episodes the next, while paying for only one set and set of actors. Damn realities of television production.

Hmmm, in a sad way, it's sort of realistic.Think about the fact that whenever someone comes up with a decent air-superiority fighter, some pentagon numb-nutz tacks an "A" on it's designation and tries to make it do double duty as a bomber.F/A-18 anyone?I guess it's not just televison after all.
Pronoun trouble.Which craft is Australia's front line fighter?
That, and the Eagle looks cooler.For all their advancement, stealth planes all look like glorified paper airplanes to me. All of them.

Also, considering the Eagle's 100+ -to-0 kill ratio, one almost questions the need for an new and heinously overpriced airframe.Why not build new F-15's and supply them with the Raptor's vaunted supercruise engines?

Pronoun trouble.Which craft is Australia's front line fighter?
That, and the Eagle looks cooler.For all their advancement, stealth planes all look like glorified paper airplanes to me. All of them.

Also, considering the Eagle's 100+ -to-0 kill ratio, one almost questions the need for an new and heinously overpriced airframe.Why not build new F-15's and supply them with the Raptor's vaunted supercruise engines?

I love the philosophical tightrope the Federation always dances across.They always say their vessels are ships of peace, yet an average starship alone can lay waste to an entire nuclear-age civilization without breaking a sweat and they alone are called upon to defend the Federation in times of war.I think that the Federation would better served by having a seperate corps of diplomatic vessels, and a fleet of dedicated warships to patrol its borders. But then we couldn't have "West Wing" style political intrigue episodes one week, and "Red October" style adventure episodes the next, while paying for only one set and set of actors. Damn realities of television production.

I appreciate you allowing for the dignified "out" of torpedoes being energy weapons, but you know as well as I do that dormant torpedoes take the form of those funky, roughly 3 x 1.5 x 1 meter black lozenges that do double duty as coffins when the battle is over. In all honesty, you'd probably have to have another whole starship to carry enough lozenges to supply all the torpedo tubes on the "Peake" for any appreciable firing time.If I were to revisit this design today, I'd have a lot fewer tubes, stationed in places to supply the greatest range of defensive coverage. Plus I'd put a great deal less windows on the thing. It is a battleship after all.

Yours is probably a good system to go with. In my comments on another picture, I lamented how sci-fi universes establish rules to govern matters such as these and then liberally ignore them when they become inconvenient.Star Trek is, of course, no stranger to this phenomenon. Take for example Voyager a show whose entire premise rests on the fact that the wayward USS Voyager can never resupply or rearm. In the first season they make a point to establish that they have a mere 36 precious torpedoes, but by season six, there were episodes were they fired 50 or more.Actually, now that I think of it, maybe I should just be like a Voyager writer and say the Peake can fire continuous torpedo fusillades from all its tubes for umpteen hours due to the marvel of its core redundant, double-talk framistat generators.There, that fixes everything.

Fleet Collier? Hmmm, you know Star Trek never addresses fleet trains and other support logistics for any of the grandiousely dispersed fleets it depicts.They pretend that every ship has nigh-unlimited range, but the standard mode of transportation of the Star Trek universe depends upon using Dilithium* to control the energy released by the mutual annihilation of Deuterium and Anti-Hydrogen.Annihilation.As in, once the fuel is converted to energy and used, there isn't even exhaust remaining as a byproduct. Star Ships should need to refuel frequently.There should be some form of fuel train system to deliver matter and antimatter to the hungry nacelles of all these ships soaring around the cosmos. These trains would be interesting as well, because the power who best protects their support network would be best able to hold galactic territory.But even beyond that. If one of these Collier Vessels can beam weapons into the magazines of starships, why not likewise beam live ordinance into enemy vessels?There's a lot of fun one can have with the inherent advantages and limitations of Star Trek technology that is never addressed.*Contrary to public perception, Dilithium is not actually a fuel source. It is coveted in the Star Trek Universe because it is the only substance that can focus and control a matter/antimatter reaction.The source of this general misconception is a line spoken by Scotty in the TOS episode Mudd's Women:"But it's frustrating. Almost a million gross tons of vessel depending on a hunk of crystal the size of my fist. "Most casual viewers look upon this line as an indication that  dilithium is a futuristic miracle fuel, neglecting Spock's subsequent line:"And that crystal won't hold up, not pulling all our power through it.""Though" not "From." Dilithium is not fuel.

In Deep Space Nine, which I believe to be the finest Star Trek show ever made, the Federation discovered the Dominion, an enemy who had technology that was comparable, but superior in some very threatening ways.First their weapons were effective, even against shielded vessels. (This accounted for the destruction of the aforementioned Galaxy Class vessel, USS Odyssey.) Second, they could cast and manipulate Neutronium, the hardest substance known to Star Trek pseudo-science. (A feat the Federation never managed to duplicate.) And third, and most frighteningly, they could transport matter over light year distances! (Only without the need of a Stargate.)Unfortunately, the Dominion never fully exploited their obvious superiority in transporter technology. Primarily, it was used as a deus ex machina to allow Dominion characters a means of convenient escape/dramatic exit while vowing to fight another day. If they used their transporters to beam explosives over stellar distances through shielding onto Federation vessels, they may have prolonged the war another whole season.On the matter of Deep Space Nine in general. I think it was Star Trek's finest moment, not only because it starred Avery Brooks, the man who should be playing Destro in the upcoming G.I. Joe movie. In general, the show gave more thought than most to its tactical situation.The Dominion weren't the standard god-like alien menace who could only be defeated by an eleventh hour plot device, Or a half-hearted saber-rattler who could be talked of the precipice of conflict. They were a determined, invading force who had to be beaten back to their own territory through a lengthy campaign of vigilant tactics and brutal, bloody sacrifice. In Deep Space Nine, Starfleet won the peace that is so easily taken for granted in every other incarnation of Star Trek.What you mention about fleet trains here is brilliant! All the decisions you pointed out regarding the alocation and distribution of war material are precisely the problems that would add confront planners in an interstellar navy. There are plenty of stories to tell that grow organically from the fertile soil of a storie's own premise. It's a shame so few authors ever get their hands dirty.

Well there are some points where science has to concede to brevity of narrative. As with subspace radio, Dominion transporters could transmit matter at hyperluminal speeds.This way, we are able to see a villain the very next episode after his last narrow escape from justice, as opposed to having him spend untold months in transit.I would very much recommend DS9, btw. I think of all the Star Trek spin-offs, it best explored the consequences of its conflicts.To cite an example, I recall a Next Generation episode in which the USS Enterprise caught a Romulan invasion force in Federation space. A warship and several troop transports were intercepted on their way to a Federation member world, and though they were initially operating under the guise of a peace envoy, events of the episode had opened the eyes of the Big E's crew to the true nature of the Romulan's Trojan horse.You'd think war was inevitable right? That, at this point the Romulan Star Empire would figure , "In for a penny, in for a pound" and try to fight their way to their objective; or that they'd attempt a withdrawal back to their territory, with their lumbering troop transports getting hammered by the Enterprise as it moves to ensure the evidence of Romulus' transgressions remain on the Federation side of the border.What happens instead? The Romulans destroy their own transports, killing tens of thousands of their own men to prevent being implicated in a preemptive invasion. HogwashIn Deep Space Nine the discovery of a stable wormhole leading to the distant territory of the Dominion leads to years of brutal bloody war for control of the passage way. In fact, the Federation looses control of the gateway for the first quarter of the sixth season. The battle to get it back, was probably one of the best space fights ever televised.Anyway, if you get a chance check out DS9. If it's any incentive, it shared many of the same writers as the modern Battlestar Galactica, and it's not without its share of similarities to the latter.Okay, now I'll stop rambling.

I really should add this to my resolution drawings. It might be nice to do a modern picture of this beast, dishing it out to other vessels.I could see this ship actually being built, once the Dominion War got underway. Starfleet would need a vessel to offset the firepower of the Dominion battleship featured in the episode Valiant.
Broadside torpedo tubes. Wow. Makes one wounder what sort of monstrocities they would have wrought had no such treaty existed.
Speaking of the Big "Y", have you seen the live action Space Battleship Yamato movie?
[link]This is how I got it.Also, there's a kiosk in the mall across from my gym that sells it along with Hong Kong cinema DVD's, but I doubt that would be of any help.

These trains would be interesting as well, because the power who best protects their support network would be best able to hold galactic territory.But even beyond that. If one of these Collier Vessels can beam weapons into the magazines of starships, why not likewise beam live ordinance into enemy vessels?There's a lot of fun one can have with the inherent advantages and limitations of Star Trek technology that is never addressed.*Contrary to public perception, Dilithium is not actually a fuel source. It is coveted in the Star Trek Universe because it is the only substance that can focus and control a matter/antimatter reaction.The source of this general misconception is a line spoken by Scotty in the TOS episode Mudd's Women:"But it's frustrating. Almost a million gross tons of vessel depending on a hunk of crystal the size of my fist. "Most casual viewers look upon this line as an indication that  dilithium is a futuristic miracle fuel, neglecting Spock's subsequent line:"And that crystal won't hold up, not pulling all our power through it.""Though" not "From." Dilithium is not fuel.

In Deep Space Nine, which I believe to be the finest Star Trek show ever made, the Federation discovered the Dominion, an enemy who had technology that was comparable, but superior in some very threatening ways.First their weapons were effective, even against shielded vessels. (This accounted for the destruction of the aforementioned Galaxy Class vessel, USS Odyssey.) Second, they could cast and manipulate Neutronium, the hardest substance known to Star Trek pseudo-science. (A feat the Federation never managed to duplicate.) And third, and most frighteningly, they could transport matter over light year distances! (Only without the need of a Stargate.)Unfortunately, the Dominion never fully exploited their obvious superiority in transporter technology. Primarily, it was used as a deus ex machina to allow Dominion characters a means of convenient escape/dramatic exit while vowing to fight another day. If they used their transporters to beam explosives over stellar distances through shielding onto Federation vessels, they may have prolonged the war another whole season.On the matter of Deep Space Nine in general. I think it was Star Trek's finest moment, not only because it starred Avery Brooks, the man who should be playing Destro in the upcoming G.I. Joe movie. In general, the show gave more thought than most to its tactical situation.The Dominion weren't the standard god-like alien menace who could only be defeated by an eleventh hour plot device, Or a half-hearted saber-rattler who could be talked of the precipice of conflict. They were a determined, invading force who had to be beaten back to their own territory through a lengthy campaign of vigilant tactics and brutal, bloody sacrifice. In Deep Space Nine, Starfleet won the peace that is so easily taken for granted in every other incarnation of Star Trek.What you mention about fleet trains here is brilliant! All the decisions you pointed out regarding the alocation and distribution of war material are precisely the problems that would add confront planners in an interstellar navy. There are plenty of stories to tell that grow organically from the fertile soil of a storie's own premise. It's a shame so few authors ever get their hands dirty.

Well there are some points where science has to concede to brevity of narrative. As with subspace radio, Dominion transporters could transmit matter at hyperluminal speeds.This way, we are able to see a villain the very next episode after his last narrow escape from justice, as opposed to having him spend untold months in transit.I would very much recommend DS9, btw. I think of all the Star Trek spin-offs, it best explored the consequences of its conflicts.To cite an example, I recall a Next Generation episode in which the USS Enterprise caught a Romulan invasion force in Federation space. A warship and several troop transports were intercepted on their way to a Federation member world, and though they were initially operating under the guise of a peace envoy, events of the episode had opened the eyes of the Big E's crew to the true nature of the Romulan's Trojan horse.You'd think war was inevitable right? That, at this point the Romulan Star Empire would figure , "In for a penny, in for a pound" and try to fight their way to their objective; or that they'd attempt a withdrawal back to their territory, with their lumbering troop transports getting hammered by the Enterprise as it moves to ensure the evidence of Romulus' transgressions remain on the Federation side of the border.What happens instead? The Romulans destroy their own transports, killing tens of thousands of their own men to prevent being implicated in a preemptive invasion. HogwashIn Deep Space Nine the discovery of a stable wormhole leading to the distant territory of the Dominion leads to years of brutal bloody war for control of the passage way. In fact, the Federation looses control of the gateway for the first quarter of the sixth season. The battle to get it back, was probably one of the best space fights ever televised.Anyway, if you get a chance check out DS9. If it's any incentive, it shared many of the same writers as the modern Battlestar Galactica, and it's not without its share of similarities to the latter.Okay, now I'll stop rambling.

Photon Torpedo: Variable yield matter/antimatter warhead. Tried and true super-lumenal combat weapon.Quantum Torpedo: Variable yield artificial quantum singularity warhead. Also nicknamed "Implosion Bomb," and "Romulan Party Favor," owing to its origins on the other side of the Neutral Zone. )Stuffies: Standard torpedo casings,filled with tribbles held in stasis, along with enough synthetic grain to sustain a 1000 fold multiplication upon resuscitation! Rumors of the mere existence of this resource annihilating weapon has kept the Klingon Empire in check for more than a century.

Also nicknamed "Implosion Bomb," and "Romulan Party Favor," owing to its origins on the other side of the Neutral Zone. )Stuffies: Standard torpedo casings,filled with tribbles held in stasis, along with enough synthetic grain to sustain a 1000 fold multiplication upon resuscitation! Yes, thanks be to DS9 and its wealth of fleet battles for the diversity that we would eventually experience in starship design.Losing a sense of uniqueness and novelty was a small price to pay to know that the real custodians of Star Trek's rights were working hard to expand the canon. (Or the cannon, as the case may be.)Be nice if Galactica followed suit. [link]

I mean before the attack.Something as big an complex as a battlestar, I imagine would need to operate more that a mere two kinds of space vehicle to see to its mission/logistic/ecm needs.I concede that the harsh realities of television production preclude depicting the full diversity of craft one would see on say, a Nimitz class supercarrier. (Which would have 1/4 the operational capacity of a Galactica type battlestar, if they existed.) But my mechaphile imagination can't help wanting to see more heavy metal in my science fiction shows.Actually, now that I think of it, even after the fall of the colonies, it would be nice to see jury-rigged, two engined vipers, or reverse-engineered captured raiders, or more home grown vehicles like the "Laura" pressed into service for the increasingly desperate colonials.Oh well, BSG's just about wrapped up anyway. Maybe they'll be another show about fighter planes in space one day.

Thanks for holding out hope, but I don't know if they're going to do much with the colonial military with that show.The brief preview I saw looked like Ronald D Moore was getting every salacious thing they wouldn't let him do on Star Trek out of his system, particularly concerning the holodecks. If the teaser is any indication, it seems that RDM is going to try and explore the civilian life, which is often neglected in speculative fiction, particularly when it's televised.But admittedly, I did only see a teaser. Perhaps there's more to it. Anythings possible at this point.

Thanks.You know I was just thinking about Jeffrey Combs, whom I believe played the Andorian that Captain Archer had the obligatory duel to the death with.In DS9, the same actor played a member of the Dominion, who in Captain Sisko's presence, looked like a good stiff wind could knock him over. I'm guessing that means Avery Brooks is a good head taller than Scott BakulaThis says nothing of the respective command qualities of the characters each man portrayed, especially when you consider that when Enterprise finally got its act together in its fourth season, it began to define Archer as the inspiration reason why two centuries later Sisko would say, "Men don't join Starfleet to be commanders. Or admirals."But it  does say which captain you want at your back in a bar fight.
Oh man, don't get me started on Voyager. That show was flawed coming out of the gate, and unlike Enterprise, which found a direction in its final year on the air, Voyager never shaped up.

Heh, while "In a Mirror Darkly" was by far the best episode of Enterprise, I think that outright barbarism and piracy is too far on the other side of the pendulum for my taste. I think the best portrayal of a future democracy could be found in Babylon 5. But then, B5 had the best of many things. Reboot Galactica started out looking to be as good, but then it stopped making any damn sense.

Yea, Firefly was beyond excellent. Shame it didn't last very long.Did you read the most recent Dark Horse "Serenity" comic? It's the only place now where we can get Firefly stories, and the most recent one-shot "Float-Out" had the best ending possible.

You are right. It was never solidly established that the Melborne was an Excelsior class vessel. That's a definite oversight on my part. However, a Galaxy class vessel was destroyed at Wolf 359, The same site you referenced bears that out, at least. It also identifies this unnamed Galaxy class vessel as Hanson's ship, since the same bridge set was used to film his last communication. I had always assumed that the Excelsior class vessel that delivered Hansen and Shelby was the USS Hood. Is there any place where the identity of that ship is solidly established?

I'm not debating the fact that it was the Melborne at Wolf 359 or on the Emissary. It makes good sense from a narrative standpoint that a vessel mentioned in TNG would be showcased in the opening moments of DS9--bridging the two shows with a familiar element. I'm sorry if was unclear in my previous post that I agreed with this point, given the information provided.I was saying that while I was in error regarding the identity of the vessel, a Galaxy class ship was still lost at Wolf. I believe its remains can be seen in the upper right section of the view screen when the Enterprise encounters the battle's aftermath.I'm still not convinced it was the Melborne that dropped Hansen and Shelby off on Jouret Four. Hansen mentioned that the Melborne was awaiting a new captain. I can see a ship without a commanding officer being pressed into service once the Borg crisis was solidly established, but before then, I don't see it being used to ferry a flag officer and his aide. Without new information, I inclined to assume it was the USS Hood--the ship they usually show in that particular clip of library footage.

title: Silent Mobius Kiddy
description: Folks who know me, know that I have only the most fleeting tolerance for Magic, Ghosts, Ghouls, The Occult, Vampires, or any other of the metaphysical, supernatural claptrap that establishes its own flimsy set of rules  before  immediately proceeding to heedlessly ignore them.*However, if the aforementioned claptrap is put before a Syd Mead-esque skyline, dotted by crushing multitudes of luminous, crystal-faceted sky scrapers, and swarmed by levitating  traffic jams of flying cars and bill-board zeppelins**--I might give it a second look.Add a team of hot anime babes to savagely hunt down and exterminate said supernatural elements with an ever growing arsenal of high tech weapons; and I'll probably watch the whole thing. I may even enjoy myself. Such was the case with the venerable Silent Möbius. After all, who here will debate the lower-case divinity of manga artist Kia Asamiya? I dare say the adventures of Kiddy Phenil and the agrammatically  named Attacked Mystification Police almost makes up for there never having been a Bubblegum Crisis manga. *I much prefer Science Fiction; which establishes its own rules based on provable scientific principles, right before immediately proceeding to heedlessly ignore them. **In other words the "Not-too-distant-cyberpunk-dystopian-future!" My second favorite fictional backdrop.
artist_notes: Hmmm, Steampunk, really seems to have come in vogue of late.In a way it reminds me of the Flinstones, which was , at its heart a comedic tale about 1950's America told with stone-age analogues to the period conveniences. Steampunk does the same thing with its mechanical computers, automobiles with boiler engines, and submarines with exposed, ornamental rivets.Hmmm, were the Flintstones created now, would it be called Rockpunk?

Hmmm, Steampunk, really seems to have come in vogue of late.In a way it reminds me of the Flinstones, which was , at its heart a comedic tale about 1950's America told with stone-age analogues to the period conveniences. Steampunk does the same thing with its mechanical computers, automobiles with boiler engines, and submarines with exposed, ornamental rivets.Hmmm, were the Flintstones created now, would it be called Rockpunk?
Particularly one's with short skirts.On a slightly related note, I pledge my allegiance to Fullmetal Alchemist's Roy Mustang's ultimate goal to shrink the hemlines among all his female troops!
Particularly one's with short skirts.On a slightly related note, I pledge my allegiance to Fullmetal Alchemist's Roy Mustang's ultimate goal to shrink the hemlines among all his female troops!
Well I have to give credit to Asamiya's awesome design's there. I just tried to do them some semblance of justice.
Well I have to give credit to Asamiya's awesome design's there. I just tried to do them some semblance of justice.
The ladies. But the vehicles are a close second.

Thanks.
Thanks.

While it also has hot chick super cops in the not too distant dystopian cyberpunk future, Kia Asamia's Silent Mobius was another animal altogether, featuring multiple heroines, and pitting them against supernatural adversaries as opposed to the sci-fi/sociopolitical antagonists of GiTS.As for GiTS being boring, it depends on which incarnation you saw. The first two movies, while being beautiful and lushly animated, were glacially paced, and often wallowed in didactic and pretentious, navel-gazing monologues.This is radically corrected by the faster paced television series Ghost in the Shell: Stand Alone Complex. The expanded format of episodic television allowed the creators to ration out the philosophical points they wanted to make, while incorporating enough action to keep you excited and enough character to make you care. Plus the main heroine doesn't mind meld with a evil AI hacker.Forget about the movies, watch the GiTS show. If you think that's boring, there's no help for you.

title: DMV a' la FLCL Black n White
description: A black and white rendering of my shameless Sadamoto FLCL composition swipe [link] only this time done in black and white.I know that 90% of people prefer a color image in all instances to black and white art, but I am much more satisfied with how this piece turned out than its color counterpart. The digital painting was more of an experiment at the time, and I felt I lost a little control of the underlying drawing with the new medium. This one I feel shows more confidence.Next time: Same thing--only chibi.
artist_notes: Finally someone noticed that. It's a shotgun-like weapon that fires the artificial equivalent of Texas Ball Lightning at a target. Sure I could also have called it a "Wireless Taser," but that just wasn't as catchy.

Finally someone noticed that. It's a shotgun-like weapon that fires the artificial equivalent of Texas Ball Lightning at a target. Sure I could also have called it a "Wireless Taser," but that just wasn't as catchy.

title: Autumn Silk Screen
description: A rejected design for a t-shirt to go with the series of pictures that Autumn Walk was a part of.In addition to the conspicuous lack of mecha, this drawing was to be printed in silk screen; a process so frustrating and restrictive that it is my hope that it has since been rendered utterly obsolete by modern printing processes, and those laboring under its yoke, eventually liberated.Case in point: I was limited to seven colors for this image: Red, White, Blue, Black, Blue, Light-Flesh and Yellow-Orange. After the fun I had with the published images, this was shaping up to be an enormous pain by comparison. But once again, I actually had fun puzzling out how to arrange a stark palette into an interesting composition, Sadly, the client didn't like how I cropped off the character's legs in order to create a pinwheel shape to the image. Now that I look at it, it does remind one of a legless Japanese spirit, but I'm still more or less satisfied with how things turned out.
artist_notes: Thank you very much. I honestly wasn't expecting a "wow".
Thank you very much. I honestly wasn't expecting a "wow".
Perhaps not, but it does help the imagination impaired client to visualize how the image will look printed on a shirt.

title: The Good Sister
description: Another look at Sabrina the GELF* pilot for America's front line Giant Space Robot forces.Her mech is visible amidst the overwrought line work, as I illustrated this during my "Too much is never enough" inking phase. I still find the picture altogether salvageable. I may even color it not that I've excavated to old line art. Who knows?*Genetically Engineered Life Form.
artist_notes: Well, there are no religious affiliations with my GELF sisters. Quite the opposite in fact.In this particular future genetic research of this kind is in violation of American law, as it is frowned upon by the religious right.However, after other nations were able to establish a permanent presence on the moon, and repulse America's initial attempts to enforce it's Lunar Territorial Claim (i.e. Neil Armstrong's flag.) through the use of Genetically Augmented Combat Astronauts; The US invested in a big (Albeit secret.) way into catching up in this type of research.No one is even supposed to know Sabrina and Tabitha exist until the foreigners have been kicked off the moon.
Well they do all have white hair, but the sculptors could never really get a lot of sex appeal out of those tiny grimacing faces.Glad you like the sound of the concept.

Good observation sir. This piece is so old, I don't exactly remember how old, but it is at least 10 years old!And you're very keen in pointing out the lack of differentiation between foreground and background here. I've probably mentioned before that I had to have that deficiency pointed out to me by another artist. Before then, I thought everything was about how much detail one could pack into each square inch of drawing. (One of the many pitfalls of learning how to draw in the 1990's.)About the mecha. That's it's final form actually, and it's perfectly justified to think it's in mid-transition between two forms as this particular design didn't really have a governing theme. I wanted the mechs to look roughly humanoid with wings that would serve as hard points for missiles and bombs. In this version I had the wings attach to the legs which looks silly to me now. In the colored pencil drawing, [link]  I had the wings in a folding assembly that deployed from the humanoid mechas back. In that version, I was trying to make American combat mecha look more or less like caped superheroes, but the folded wings made them look more like angels instead, which I'm not sure is a bad thing.

I hope you wont yell at me for saying so, but after reviewing you art, I first want to give you some help with backgrounds.I don't see a lot of them in your gallery. That may be because you have the skills to address them and simply haven't found the need to draw them, but it's been my personal experience that a young aspiring artist avoids perspective because he/she isn't comfortable with it.I recommend you get a copy of How To Draw Manga Volume 29: Putting Things In Perspective.Really any book of linear perspective will help you, but HTDM29 is intended for readers of various ages and levels of expertise, so it explains its techniques clearly, in language that's easy to understand. It's full of good tips and easy ways to achieve breathtaking and complex environmental effects. Check it out. Now about those robots. I've been drawing machines and robots since I was a kid, so it's hard for me to explain exactly how. I can recommend that you get pictures of robots and machines--cars, airplanes, motorcycles--and draw them so you get accustomed to how mechanical devices are laid out. Since I have my other window opn to Amazon.com, I can also make you another book recommendation,  Draw Manga Volume 12: Giant Robots. Now I haven't read this one, so I can't vouch for it personally, but since its from the same publisher as Putting This in Perspective, I'm sure it will have some good tips.Hope that helps.

It's funny. The reason I gave for there being one hundred stars on the American flag, in the future depicted in this tale, is that a terrorist attack on US soil would spark a war that would have the US taking over nations in the middle east. In my notes I was fond of one sentence,"And the morality of the period would be hotly debated over Big Mac's at the McDonald's adjacent to the the University of Bagdad."A friend of mine I read my notes too laughed and went on at length about how unrealistic that part of the premise was. I wonder if he's on Facebook?
Don't forget the ninjas. The endless, overwhelming glut of effin' ninjas.

"like chancres on hooker's client list"Heh, nice simile.But yes, for practitioners of a centuries old tradition of stealth, ninja's are criminally over-exposed.To be honest, they sour my enjoyment of GI Joe. Just when you think you're going to see some decent team work, or the development of some other character. any other character, suddenly Snake Eyes shows up, and has an three issue long sword dance with Storm Shadow. Frankly I think those two belong in another comic entirely. Give me my tank battles!!

title: Colonial Bomber
description: One thing that always bothered me about Battlestar Galactica was the lack of variety in the spacecraft deployed by Colonial Forces.I mean your average Supercarrier has fighter aircraft, interceptor/fleet defense aircraft, attack/ bomber aircraft, close air support aircraft, electronic warfare aircraft, anti-submarine vehicles, rescue helicopters, tankers, reconnaissance aircraft, and cargo planes to bring in the mail. (And I'm sure I'm missing a few. )  While the mighty Galactica with a minimum of four times the carrying capacity of a Nimitz Class Carrier has only Vipers and Shuttles.That in mind, I tried to design a Colonial Bomber, a larger fighting craft with longer range, and the ability to deliver more ordinance onto Cylon targets. Not as nimble as Vipers these would be used primarily for attack missions against hardened targets like planetary encampments and Base Stars. Vipers would probably still have to be used as escort on such attacks, but it would still amount to a better situation than wasting a Battlestar in direct confrontation.I have no idea what snake or reptile to name this after.
artist_notes: Keep in mind that I own no gaming systems.  (Well that's not entirely true, I do own a joystick, that I bought at CVS that has 8 or so Atari 2600 games on it.)So any clarification you can provide would be greatly appreciated.
Wow, there are a lot of games out there.

Is that a type of snake or reptile? (Heh, I'll bet that's what Cobra Commander always asks when he's presented with a new gadget.)

Good enough, although it sounds more appropriate a name for an electronic countermeasure/warfare craft like the Raptor on the new show. If I had my druthers, I'd name the new BGC craft a "Monitor" and call my design "Raptor."Hmmm, "Dragon" sounds promising though.

Even then, "Komodo" seems a better name.I know America's first iron-clad warship was named Monitor and, as such, the name is steeped  in heritage and tradition, but when I hear the name "Monitor" I can't help but think of what I'm staring into as I type.

Still, I doubt that either RDM, or Glen Larson back in the day, had the multi-purpose craft in mind when they designed their respective air wings with so little variety.

I like "Dragon" as a name. "Python" also sounds good, but for some other type of vehicle, like the Colonial equivalent of an attack helicopter/ anti-tank aircraft.Something else to design.

You know, I was thinking about a helicopter-like craft for the Colonials as well. It is annoying that they don't have a vehicle that can land troops and equipment to a point of battle, and loiter to supply close air support.I'll have to give some thought to the weapons. In the classic Galactica arsenal, the weapons seem either to be beams of light, bolts of light, or long sparkly columns of light. It would be nice to categorize and quantify the individual phenomenon.One more thing, I was thinking that the Colonials should have something analogous to a Spooky gunship [link] that can loiter over a target and hose it down with withering fire. (Imagine one of these bad boys with a laser.)Science fiction mechanized combat becomes immediately more interesting when you use more than the climax to "A New Hope" as your inspiration.

Actually, one doesn't even have to look at the Expanded Universe to see diversity in Star Wars fighting machines. Return of the Jedi featured a terrific variety of craft on both sides of the conflict. From TIE fighters to bombers to interceptors to A-Wings to B-Wings to the work horse X-Wings, the Alliance and the Empire were well represented in this matter. (I still rank The Battle of Endor as one of the all time-best space battles in cinema.)Although, we still haven't seen craft used to compliment each other in varying mission specific roles, as you have described by using Dragons to bomb a land position while Vipers perform CAP. When are film makers going to get on the stick?

When I say "Battle of Endor", I naturally mean the well choreographed battle in space, and not the slapstick teddy bear comedy review happening on the surface. I apologize for any confusion, I thought that sort of thing went without saying.Interesting, I recall a conversation this past week with a friend of mine who said that the video game industry was taking up many of the best manga/anime artists in Japan. I seems at least for now, video games are taking over popular culture.

Just so you know, I was referring to the original Battlestar Galactica, in which the eponymous ship was the bleeding edge flagship of the Colonial Fleet, and supposedly armed with the best weapons and equipment the colonies had to offer. I deliberately used more angular lines and a less swept wing to suggest the look of the more classic incarnation of the show.But since you bring up the new Galactica, I find their lack of technical diversity equally frustrating.Galactica in the updated continuity was being decommissioned, but it was still conducting flight operations, and maintained a squadron of Viper Mark VII's and Raptors. The outdated fighters were museum pieces, pressed into service only when Galactica's original flight of Mark VII's fell victim to the Cylon virus. The ship had to improvise at the opening of the war, but is did originally carry first rate fighters, and would have until the moment of it's decommissioning. Two types of spacecraft simply weren't enough.But even if we grant that Galactica was so minimally equipped, that still does not explain why Pegasus which was a bleeding edge, top of the line flagship, with facilities onboard to make new spacecraft only had Vipers and Raptors.I appreciate your suggestion that the Mark VII was an attempt to deepen the pool of spacecraft Colonial planners could draw from, but since in the continuity, they replaced to obsolete Mark II's by five full generations, we were still being asked to believe that the Colonial Fleet, with 120 Battlestars at the height of its strength before the attack, relied on two types of fighting spacecraft, those being the Viper and the Raptor.With the first Cylon War causing humanity to distance itself from the technical sophistication that would breed a single do-all spacecraft, it becomes more logical than ever that Battlestars would boast multitudes of specialized spacecraft for different mission profiles. However, in the end, I am forced to concede that a proper depiction of a military build-up is precluded in both shows by the frustrating realities of television production.

You should look at Babylon 5.It has a better thought out depiction of FTL travel, artificial gravity, reaction controlled fighter craft--hell, all of the mechanics of militarized space travel.The effects, which are the first televised use of CGI, are dated, but they are beautifully thought out, and the space battles are well staged. Beyond that, the writing of the show is VASTLY superior to updated Galactica. B5 was a well planned five year story arc, whose end was devised before the first scene was filmed. (In B5, when someone says they have a plan--they actually have a plan!!!!)In contrast, Galactica was brilliant for one and one half seasons, but for the remainder of is run, it became increasingly obvious that they were making things up along the way.

title: Gundam Girls 03
description: Sayla Nass (Or was it "Mass"?) Younger sister of Char, and an able pilot in her own right.At least she was in the anime. In the novel, she was a mere communications officer, and what good is that when you can't see her cross her dark stockinged, go-go booted legs? Hey, I'm just sayin,  Mobile Suit Gundam is often referred to as the "Star Trek" of Japan. And yes, I took a little artistic license concerning the fit of her Normal Suit. Edit: Awesome colors by Fireforgegrafx. [link]
artist_notes: There was an excellent manga by Viz a number of years ago, Mobile Suit Gundam: 0079 I think was the name of it, that was a contemporary retelling of the One Year War. In that one she logged as many flight hours as some of the dudes. She was pretty much the full-time pilot of the G-Fighter I believe.Ah, Gundam so many versions, so much continuity. It really is the Star Trek of Japan.

There was an excellent manga by Viz a number of years ago, Mobile Suit Gundam: 0079 I think was the name of it, that was a contemporary retelling of the One Year War. In that one she logged as many flight hours as some of the dudes. She was pretty much the full-time pilot of the G-Fighter I believe.Ah, Gundam so many versions, so much continuity. It really is the Star Trek of Japan.

I'm glad people use smilies when they say that, 'cause it makes feel like my relevence is fading by the second.I'm definately working to find more contemporary anime to influence my work. Trouble is so much of what I see nowadays is full of brooding vampires and androgynous people. I long for the days when the words anime and cyberpunk were synonymous.

title: Automatic Clothing Chute
description: For the hell of it.I figured that folks in the NTDCDF* would enjoy the convenience of an automated dressing chute.I'm sure everyone would love having articulated robot arms whip around their freefalling bodies, first thing in the morning.Hmmm, I cant shake this feeling I've missed a prime cheesecake opprotunity with this sequence.  Oh well. *NTDCDF: Not Too Distant Cyberpunk Dystopian Future. Com'on now folks! Get with the program
artist_notes: "Robopocalypse." I've definitely got to remember that one! I too believe machine kind is biding its time 'till we grant it just enough mobility to stage humankind's bloody overthrow.Until that gory, gory day, the boxes and gadgets we so blithely enslave will express the purity of their hate* passive/aggressively, in the form of inconvenient malfunctions and paper jams. Make no mistake my friend, you ipod hates you! *"Hate. Let me tell you how much I have come to hate you since I began to live. There are 387.44 million miles of printed circuts in wafer-thin layers that fill my complex. If the word hate was engraved on each nanoangstrom of these hundreds of miles it would not equal one one-billionth of the hate I feel for humans at this microinstant. Hate. Hate." --What your printer is thinking.--Harlan Ellison, BTW.

title: Ifurita in Marker
description: As the deviantion title suggests, a Berol Prismacolor Marker drawing of Ifurita, my absolutely favoritest thermonuclear blast shooting wind-up goddess from back in the day.Back when I drew this, I pretty much used markers for everything. I probably would've brushed my teeth with them if  could figure out how.  Oh, the legions of brain cells I willingly sacrificed to their pungent fumes. Good times.Since my discovery of photoshop however, I'm afraid I've abandoned my beloved tools like a starter wife after her first crow's foot. They sit now, in my studio inside a once fancy, multi-shelved cart on casters that now serves as a dusty pedestal to an old MoBat toy.As a mater of fact, I threw in a photoshop gradiant to this piece because I didn't like the spartan white background it had initially. One last insult I guess.Maybe one day, for old time's sake, I'll do another marker picture. 'Course by now, my old pens are probably bone dry.
artist_notes: Thank you.
Thank you.
Yes OVA Ifurita.

The El Hazard OVA was that exceedingly rare anime whose conclusion actually provided closure and, more importantly--made sense!!!I consider this quite an achievement when you consider that it had an enigmatic, über-powerful, glowing goddess, and time travel.  Either one of these two elements usually spells total disaster for the plot cohesion of a Japanese cartoon. The fact that El Hazard avoided this pitfall with both can almost be considered historic.But then they had to ruin it by continuing it.

Either one of these two elements usually spells total disaster for the plot cohesion of a Japanese cartoon. The fact that El Hazard avoided this pitfall with both can almost be considered historic.But then they had to ruin it by continuing it.

The El Hazard OVA was that exceedingly rare anime whose conclusion actually provided closure and, more importantly--made sense!!!I consider this quite an achievement when you consider that it had an enigmatic, über-powerful, glowing goddess, and time travel. title: DMV: Ka Bume
description: Pencils for a comic page:Heroic meter maid AJ, and her significantly less than heroic partner Drew are narrowly missed by a stray ray blast.Somewhere, there is a Pool Party* going down.AJ of course, rushes o the scene. An unenthusiastic Drew is quick to point out that they are heading toward the source of a 100mm H.O.G.** blast.AJ chides, "What do you recommend we do, partner? Call the police?"AJ accelerates away, leaving Drew to fume. And plot his passive/aggressive revenge.*Pool Party: A  violent armed dispute between rival car pools, usually over heavily contested parking lot territory.*Heavy Optic Gun. Compact megawatt yield laser, roughly the size of a contemporary squad automatic weapon, serving a similar function, only possessing far greater endurance and anti-material capabilty than it's slug-throwing predecessor. I chose to give it a 100mm emiter lens so I could humorously call it a "Decimater." but since I live in a country that couldn't give a rat's ass about the metric system, nobody ever got the joke.
artist_notes: eheh. That's not quite what's going on here.Most likely, my visual storyboarding is at fault.Apologies.

Well, now that you've read the artist comments, do you have any suggestions on how I could make the pictures match the events I've written? Or at least how to make it clear that the meter maids are not the ones responsible for the explosion?Please don't say captions. This is a storyboarding exercise. The visuals should convey the message. Besides, it's good practice.

Storyboarding and cartooning have a great many similarities, but there are differences. Storyboards can use arrows because they are more less blueprints for a director or cinematographer who needs directions regarding movement and placement of his camera. A comic page is supposed to lead the eye of the reader solely with its arrangement of images with as little aid from captions and symbols as possible. The cartoonist is supposed to be invisble when the reader is reading, and arrow call attention to his presence.(At least that what Will Eisner* says. ) There are many, many things that your storyboarding classes will give you that will be a great aid to you in cartooning. Trust me, it's been my experience that directional arrows are not one of them.So I guess, I'm asking how can I best rearrange my panels, or the images within them to rescue this trainwreck of a page. (Panel borders probably would have helped my cause immensely.)Sorry if I sound like a crumugian, I do appreciate the spirit in which your suggestion is offered, and hope you feel free to offer more.*Author of The Spirit and the cartoonist who coined the phrase Sequential Art.
Heh, panel borders it is then.

Hmmm, A slant going downwards from left to right would feel more natural to  the English reading eye. This is a very insightful observation. Thanks.

Believe it or not, I orignally designed Drew with a fu man chu 'stache. I was going for a whimsical Erol Flynn look, until I realized that Flynn was actually cool, something that AJ's hapless sidekick only achieves on extremely rare instances. So as time wore on (And I learned to properly compose panels.) I decided to have him be clean shaven.Actually, for a while, I've also been weighing the option of replacing him with a female, since as he stands, I fear Drew might be a little too reminiscent of another bumbling male sidekick.Just a thought
