title: Cho Mama
description: Brandy Carter* from Liberty Meadows* done in my faltering approximation of a manga style. She is posing here on a Motoslave from Bubblegum Crisis. (Hmmmm. the fifth Knight Sabre?)I was always impressed by the cleverness inherent in Frank's granting of Brandy's last name. Everybody already knows she is based on the legendary Lynda Carter, but the surname "Carter" also allows him to pay homage to Edgar Rice Burrows by simply naming her father John.I always thought that was neat. But then I've always been easy to impress.Inks by Frank Cho.*Brandy Carter and Liberty Meadows are © F. Duk Cho.
artist_notes: Hah!So you're the one who read that article.
Hah!So you're the one who read that article.

Sorry my friend. My first two responses to this question where laden with sarcasm at your expense. I sometimes forget that cultural phenoms we take for granted in the US aren't always circulated worldwide.Lynda Carter is an incredibley beautiful television actress who played Wonder Woman in a phenomenally popular television series that aired from 1975 to 1979. [link] Beyond filling out the costume well  Lynda Carter had a charismatic screen presence that exemplified all aspects of the amazing amazon's character so perfectly that she is strongly associated to the character to this day.Any boy in the United States alive between 1975 and 1979 worships Lynda Carter. Heh, I remember after enthusiastically recounting a episode to a friend on the playground unfortunate enough to have missed it, a nearby girl, in an obvious fit of jealously over our doting on Ms. Carter sniped,"Why don't you just marry Wonder Woman?"I don't think the fact that my friend and I soon engaged into a dusty, wrestly kid-fight over who gets to marry Wonder Woman did anything to assuage  her jealousy. Since here, Wonder Woman's iconic status is far, FAR greater than that of Storm's, I assumed that you would be familiar with the character, her history, and her incarnations in the various media, such as the famed television show. This in mind, I assumed that you were joking with your question, and responded in kind. Sorry about that.I also remembered that you are from Germany. It is entirely possible that the show may not even have aired in your country, since the first season took place during WWII, and Ms. Carter spent much of her time stage-punching bad Hollywood actors who where engaging in even worse negative German stereotypes. During the first half of the second season, the show switched its setting to present day, (Taking full advantage of the character's immortality to save the production costs of recreating a bygone era.) however, I doubt her battles with Neo-Nazis and a Hitler clone would have made her any more a desirable option for modern German broadcasting.Excuse my insensitivity, and I hope this all answers your question.

No worries. And never apologize for geekiness. Now I do feel I should clarify one thing. Lynda Carter didn't "fight" stereotypes like an activist, promoting a positive image of Germans, or anything like that. She played opposite people portraying stereotypical characters, and her character was locked in mortal combat with theirs.So when I say she "fought stereotypes", I mean she wore an American flag leotard with a golden eagle for a bra and pretended beat the stuffing out of wicked trolls in SS uniforms who spoke English with crummy accents. Sadly, if anything, the Wonder Woman show may well have reinforced the very stereotypes you dislike.This could be why the show didn't air in your country.Sorry, I'd like you to like Lynda Carter as much as me and my friends, but I don't feel right having you assign her a merit she didn't possess. I'm not saying she's even remotely a Teutophobe, but neither was she an activist. She was a competent actress who was built like a brick ship-house, and that is primarily why we love her so.
Good to hear.Sorry to ramble. Sometimes I just love the sound of my own typing.

title: More American Streamers
description: Another maker colored group shot of the winsome super soldiers I created.This was actually created earlier than the picture I posted previously. The design for Air Force Girl here was, I felt, too similar to AJ from my DMV concept. I don't  know why I should concern myself with the similarities between two characters of my own creation, particularly when neither has seen the light of day publicationwise; but  I decided to design Air Force Girl anyway to how she appears in the aforementioned previous post. The big rockety thing she's strapped into is an Orbital Rig, allowing her to do anti-satellite warfare. It was inspired more that a little by some of Ace McCloud's gear from the cartoon Centurians. (Man the 1980's truly were the golden age of cartoons where stuff blowed up.)
artist_notes: Probably.It would be fun to revisit some of these concepts. Perhaps even redesign some elements.Marine Girl for instance, I was considering dressing in a body-hugging, cropped-top version of USMC's trademark dress blues. Although that probably would make her look too much like one of the Strike Witches, despite having human legs. And without all the decorative ribbons flowing from their costumes, there would be little point in calling them Streamers.Still, the idea of super-heroines going toe-to-toe with heavy Warsaw Pact arms and equipment, is simply to appealing for me to indefinitely abandon. It would be fun to at least generate a pin-up or two in my contemporary style.

I'll definitely have to draw these characters again soon, although I wonder how much of the old school look I'll be able to recreate.

title: Ka POW
description: A little fan-boy wish fulfillment from one of my favorite old animes.To those of you who recall Neon Genesis Evangelion, remember the episode when Shinji got swallowed uo by that Angel that kind of looked like a Rover?*Remember the scene when Asuka was talkin' all kinds of heartless smack about Shinji, as he suffered in the bottomless sea of who knows where? Remember when an obviously pissed Rei got up in her face?This is what I really would like to have seen happen next!Anyway, this is yet another ancient marker drawing that I unearthed. Please excuse the overall cringeworthiness of this old piece. As an anime fan, as well as the artist responsible, I kind of felt a duty to post it.* Prisoner reference. R.I.P. Patrick McGoohan.
artist_notes: Heh, yea I just hadda add those in.
Heh, yea I just hadda add those in.

I enjoyed NGE because its biblical refences weren't gratuitous as was the case in the multitudes of shameless imitators it would spawn. They were integral to the story, and a delight for anyone whose ever had to study the book of Revelations. However, if such references aren't your cup of tea on principle, I can see where NGE wouldn't be your thing. Not everything is for everyone. For instance, gothic vampire anime just turn me off. It would have to be particularly well done for to get me to endure yet another tale of tortured soul Fabio in a poofy pirate shirt.

Yea, NGE is not without its pacing problems. Especially at the end.Structurally Evangellion is patterned after Patrick McGoohan's famed 1960's psychedelic adventure show The Prisoner. There are even many references sprinkled throughout the anime: such as the punching of Shinji Ikari's ID card in exactly the same manner as McGoohan's character's in the opening of the Prisoner. Both show's also follow a pattern of a decreasingly linear narrative in which the story literally flies apart into a flurry of indecipherable stream of consciousness at the end, featuring, in both cases, scenes of apocalyptic destruction, to the tune of chirpy Beatles' music.The funny thing is that, also in both cases, the writers had to go into hiding when their respective viewerships flew into incandescant rages at the avante guard nature of each conclusion. Considering that he would obviously be aware of the negative reaction to McGoohan's work 30 years earlier, you'd figure Hideki Anno would have known better. Or a least have taken better precautions.

Considering that he would obviously be aware of the negative reaction to McGoohan's work 30 years earlier, you'd figure Hideki Anno would have known better. Or a least have taken better precautions.

title: American Streamers Pencils 01
description: I really like the old Mil Mi-24 attack helicopter. Few machines serve better as a visual cue, announcing "Here come the bad guys!" than this ass-ugly, flying crocodile. (I'm actually surprised and disappointed that Hasbro™ never co-opted the design for use in their G.I.Joe™ toy line--It would make a perfect Cobra™ vehicle!)Anyway, the purpose of this sequence was to demonstrate how woefully inadequate conventional weapons are against Tactical Combat Maidens, or Streamers.I believe Marine Girl fatally headbutts the hapless pilot on her way through the fuselage of the doomed chopper.I really need to find that page.
artist_notes: Wow that was quick. Thank you very, very much.
Wow that was quick. Thank you very, very much.

Man, gotta say the F-14 is THE sexiest fighter ever--period. I'm sure the FA-18 and the F-35 are more technically advanced and all that but man, they just don't look anywhere near as cool.

I'm glad to discover I actually did have a copy made of the aforementioned picture. Learning sonething of the USMC mystique from my friend, I decided it would be appropriate to give her a rifle. title: Ms. Claus
description: Yet another old drawing. The real irony is, I found it when putting away the Christmas tree. (That's right, I said "putting away" as in "artificial" Don't you judge me, man! ) And yes, it is too late to put up a holiday themed drawing, but after cutting myself on a splinter getting this out of a cold cramped basement, I'm not in the mood to wait 347 days to post it.Of the drawing itself, its of a young nubile daughter of jolly St. Nick along with high tech reindeer, whose design should be instantly recognizable to Galaxy Rangers fans.Yea, I know I should have finished the thing, but I guess I was crestfallen when I learned Paul Dini had come up with the same idea. (Except for the robot reindeer of course.)
artist_notes: No surprise you're a GR fan. You like everything cool.
No surprise you're a GR fan. You like everything cool.

title: The Other Type of Valkyrie
description: Many clicking on this link may well be expecting another drawing of a Valkyrie Fighter Jet from Super Dimension Fortress Macross/Robotech, which I promise you will see more of eventually.This particular valkyrie is Skogul, the love interest in Mike Avon Oemings "Hammer of the Gods," a comic book I used to color waaaay back when all we needed to research Norse mythology was look out our windows.This character is entirely why, while I was flattered to get an actual drawing published, I still lamented getting tasked with the previously posted page containing the snaggled-toothed Frost Giant Matriach.[link]I should have been clearer when I said I wanted to draw one of the female characters.
artist_notes: Yea, but in the end I suupose it was for the best to show that I could draw men as well as women, and that I could draw more kinds of women than zaftig supermodels.But still...

title: Chiroines 05
description: Chibified Batgirl. And here your thought I would never get back to the ancient promised series of diminutive super-heroine pictures. Truth be told, this actually predates my other Chiroine pictures, (I think this was from when I was actually using a brush to ink--Kudos to all you lower case gods who've acquired a proficiency at that odious task.) but considering the nature of the drawing, it felt appropriate to consider it among the bourgening collection.
artist_notes: Ah, a DC.With so many Marvel devotees about, it's nice to find someone who appreciates the classics.
Yea, I always found DC's characters to be more iconic.

title: American Streamers
description: I just found this old Prismacolormarker drawing, mere moments ago, and now, due to my having joined the 21st century and acquiring a modern computer, I am able to post it before I think better of it.The concept behind this drawing is one of my more bizarre one's, so bear with me:After watching way to many anime in which a Nimitz Class carrier is sent to Davey Jones' Locker by a teeny bopper in a sailor fuku, I decided to concoct a story in which super-powered sailor girls become the chief expression of strategic military power.American strategic military power.In the back story, the ability to endow young women with extraordinary abilities is pioneered by the Japanese toward the end of the second world war. Due to the shortages of manpower brought about by the conflict, Japanese scientists desperately work to develop a process that would allow it's remaining female population to defend the home island from the anticipated American invasion. In order to halt the American advance, a means has to be developed to allow a single woman to wipe out an armed division of battle hardened troops. In many ways, this would be Japan's own Manhattan project.As it would turn out, the process proves far too expensive and resource intensive for the Japanese to fully execute, and only a blueprint for it is completed by war's end. Along with the coveted secrets of German rocketry and jet technology, The so-called Tokyo Project comes to number among the United State's many scientific spoils of war.After decades of additional research, a process is successfully developed to, for the price of a mere 1.5 billion dollars each, give a young woman1, the ability to fly, hurl tanks, breath in space, punch through steel, and walk on the ocean floor. Basic "Superman" stuff.Inter-service politics being what they are, each branch of the military wanted their own:Clockwise from the top:Air Force Girl (Blue and White uniform.) Flight Ceiling: 95,000 feet. Top speed: Mach 10. Invulnerability to rigors of unassisted supersonic flight. Hands generate gigawatt-class laser bursts. Limited strength.Navy Girl (Gold and White uniform.) Flight Ceiling : 75,000 feet. Top Flight Speed: Mach 6. Maximum Submerged Speed: 50 Knots. Maximum Dive Depth: 30,000 feet. Invulnerable to rigors of flight and deep sea travel. Hands generate megawatt-class laser bursts. Limited strength.Marine Girl (Black and Red uniform. ) Flight Ceiling: 50,000 feet. Top Flight Speed: Mach 2. Maximum Submeged Speed: 30 Knots. Maximum Dive Depth 20,000 feet. 20 Kiloton proof invulnerability. Unable to generate laser bursts( Uses M256 cannon, coverted for use as assault rifle.) 45,000 ton deadlift strength.Army Girl: (Green and Brown uniform)No flight ability. No undersea ability. Maximum Speed Overland: 110mph.3 Megaton Proof invulnerability. Unable to generate laser bursts. Uses P.O.R.T.I.A.2 grenades (Pictured.) as well as M256 rifle. 88,000 ton deadlift strength.The Jet behind them is the Battle Queen. Essentially, it's Army Girl's ride to the fight. Together these girls are called the American Streamers. And no, there isn't a Coast Guard Girl. I really liked this idea, but I suppose, the publication fo Image's War heroes has rendered all this pretty much moot. But then, my idea is ten years older and a whole lot less cynical, so perhaps there's a place for it afterall. Who knows? 1.) Why women? Because they're more fun to draw.2.) Posable Ordinance-grade Resin Ten Inch Action-figure.
artist_notes: Yea, it's been a while since I did a marker drawing. Any opinion on the concept? Like I said, discussion may well be moot, but I'm still mildly curious to hear what people think.

Yea,and they can lift aircraft carriers. (Well, Army Girl anyway.)One of the things I wanted was to have them used with military tactics. Instead of having Captain America do his Clark Kent impression by wandering through the war in the guise of Private Steve Rogers, until he stumbles upon the Red Skull's latest weapon, Streamers would be deployed in conjunction with other military operations.Troops would be able to call air support from Airforce Girl, or Marine Girl could go behind enemy lines and snipe out entire anti-aircaft emplacements in advance of a bombing attack.A school of thought or combat doctrine would evolve around the deployment of tactical maidens, like dogifighting for military aviation, or blitzkreg tactics for mobile armor.Rather than telling a superhero story masquerading as a war story, I wanted to tell a science fiction story about how a new technology changes the face of combat.So I guess you could say it's like Captain America if they took the idea as far as it could go, and had more than one super soldier. And they were all cute girls.
Voogie Angel are part of the American military? I have to see this anime?
Well I'll take a look anyway.

Yea, it's usually: The government is a bunch of mell-intended buffoons who only deploy super-soldiers when the incompetant and/or superfluous bulk of their forces are swept aside by the villain of the week; or the government is a creepy iluminati who seeks to use super-powered beings toward some unknowable, sinister end, making it necessary for the requisite super-powered "teens-with-attitude" to take it on the lam.Even War Heroes, which I thought at first would be like Streamers, is just about a bunch of traitorous thugs who sell their powers to terrorists.Perhaps there's a place for this idea after all.

Sometimes the best place to start is to take a look at a story you've read or seen, but were not satisfied with the ending. If you can determine how you would end it, you already are telling a story.Fan fiction, taking the characters and circumstances from a story you enjoy, and expanding upon them is good practice for an someone trying to get some practice. After a fashion you might become comfortable inventing your own characters.Have fun with it. It's not as hard as you might think. ')

It seems to me that you're quite adept at making stories. And you really like cross-dressing boys. (Oh Shoujo Manga, you corrupt or youth in ways Frederick Wertham never even imagined. )"macho but still sweet,"?You know what? That sounds a damn sight better than "Old School", which is what I usually hear from folks.Thanks.

Well thank you for your kind words. I certainly hope my work is not in any way responsible for your headache, and I hope you feel better soon.I suppose over time, the Streamer program would be expanded, so there would eventually be a Coast Guard Girl. But what would her power set be. She would probably have limited flight, undersea capability, and certain degrees of invulnerability.She really wouldn't need weapons, so she'd have no hand lasers, and it's doubtful she'd be issued and artillery rifle. However, she'd probably have super strength, to allow her to lift damaged ships out of danger.Despite her powers, she's still only Coast Guard.  The others will probably give her a hard time.

I just had a thought.In all the shoujo mecha shows, (Which my concept predates my more than a decade, ironically enough.) there is a gateway character, who serves as the lens through which the audience is introduced to the world.She is universally short haired, and lacking in any outstanding talents. She's not as busty as the mandatory bombshell, she's nicer than the mean girl, though not as nice as the gentle shy girl. In general, she's more or less a blank template, there to bring the special traits of the actually interesting characters into higher relief, by means of contrast.This could be an ideal role for Coast Guard Girl.Crap, I may actually have to start doing something with this idea.

Also, Marine Girl and Army Girl will be total Tsundere Friends. They will constantly bicker, leveraging the ongoing Army/USMC rivalry that my service friends and house mates have borne out over the years. They'll always be in one another's face until one of them is in a jam. Navy Girl will be the snotty, rich one. I guess that leaves Air Force Girl as the brainy one. I need to redesign her with some "Royal Smart Person" glasses.

Now for their foe.Their enemy will be something that conventional forces are powerless against.An evil scientist is trying to force her version of peace upon the world by activating a series of devices, whihc turn everything within their range of influence into the most adorable, chibified version of itself; Houses smiling with winking window eyes, anthropomorphic cars that bounce along on inner tube tires, dancing to songs sung by cherry trees with cherub cheeks. Never ending, sugar coated horror.All armies entering the Quarantine Transformation Zones, or QTZ's, are subject to these horrific, disfigurements, and only add to the strength of forces bent on building new devices and spreading the scourge of super-deformation globally.fav.me/d1q20xcBasically leveraging my love of darwing SD hardware.Only the Streamers can enter the QTZ, destroy the field generators, and free those trapped within. So once again, the worlds holds its breath, and waits for America to come save its ass.

Hah! Japan's answer to Captain America! This sounds like a menace for "season two". Of course, as far as anyone in the Streamers program knows, Japan produced no working Tactical Girls before turning their research over to the US. I'm sure the revelation of the existence of Shoujo-Zero would be quite an embarrassment for the JSDF. They may even call upon the American unit too late to save a major city of land mark from destruction; opting to attempt to contain her with Anti-Tactical Maid Weapons, developed in secret, as a countermeasure against the Streamers.The Streamers would be livid at the JSDF, right up until the moment they realize that the ATMW's actually came from the US.Starting as an adversary, the Streamers eventually realize that each of them could have been Shoujo-Zero under different circumstances. Of course this revelation will not changer the fact that SZ will have to be put down, for the greater good.Season Two would definitely be darker, featuring more intrigue, and higher character stakes than the QT War story arc.
BBEG?

Ah. I thought this was in reference to a specific anime, as was you mention of DBZ.I'm still brimming with ideas for this concept now. The opening should definitely involve the destruction of a Nimitz class carrier. (The fact that this happens so much in anime is what inspired Streamers to begin with.)A carrier group will be standing off shore in support of a special forces operation, launched to capture a rogue evil scientist. (I was going to call her Doctor Genius, but a quick google search revealed that name was taken. ) The troops dispatch the expected crowd of faceless hench dudes, and proceed to arrest the mad doctor, who is disquietingly okay with the whole affair.Soldier: "Come along quietly, doctor. We've taken care of your private little army. It's over!"Doctor: "Quiet the contrary, soldier. My army's just arrived."No sooner do the soldiers grab the doctor than her first Deformation Generator activates, turning everything, and everyone, on or around the island (Except for the doc herself, who as made herself immune to its effects with a special process that turned her skin a delightful shade of lavender.) into the most adorable, super deformed versions of themselves.Now with the might of history's most huggable carrier battle group, the maniacal doctor moves her plan of global conquest forward.Two additional thoughts.1.) One of the special forces soldiers initially confronting the dread doctor could be the future Special Forces Girl. Of course this raises the additional question of whether SFG is immune at first, of if she's liberated by the Streamers to later add to their ranks, with a thirst for vengeance created by her time as a super deformed thrall.2.) After watching Nichijou, I am wondering if I could get away with making the BBEG into a little girl, like Professor Hakase Shinonome from the anime, Nichijou? Would explain the obsession with cuteness.

These are some amazing suggestions!!! It hadn't occurred to me to have Special Forces Girl be covered up in any way, but that would give her that air of mystery like Snake Eyes from G.I. Joe or The Stig from Top Gear, or Raim Kawasaki from Bakuon, and I'm sure there are a multitude of examples I am not aware of. Having a long standing mystery regarding her appearance, before it is finally revealed that see is a survivor of the first deformation, sounds very satisfying, along with the big reveal that she hides herself because of the surreal beauty that is now her eternal curse.The idea of the chibified Aegis cruisers and Nimitz class carriers having the weakness of pool toy valves is inspired. In addition to just being plain cute and funny, it's a nice reference to the mandatory "weak point" that seems to afflict all genre monsters. And yes, the super deformation effect and the Streamer process, do share a similar technical origin. The evil doctor may well have been a high ranking scientist for the program, until her experiments with rejuvenation curse her with a skull too small for her mighty brain. In fact, it could even be possible that our widdle doctor, (Hmmm, Doctor Widdle? ) once cured of her rejuvenation, could revert back to her old self and work towards redemption by helping the Streamers against Shoujo Zero.Army Girl is afraid of heights, actually, but I really do need to give more thought to the various quirks of the other girls.
Plus, she stays cute.
Finally created her.
That sounds more like something more appropriate for Special Forces Girl.

I just had a thought.In all the shoujo mecha shows, (Which my concept predates my more than a decade, ironically enough.) there is a gateway character, who serves as the lens through which the audience is introduced to the world.She is universally short haired, and lacking in any outstanding talents. She's not as busty as the mandatory bombshell, she's nicer than the mean girl, though not as nice as the gentle shy girl. In general, she's more or less a blank template, there to bring the special traits of the actually interesting characters into higher relief, by means of contrast.This could be an ideal role for Coast Guard Girl.Crap, I may actually have to start doing something with this idea.

Also, Marine Girl and Army Girl will be total Tsundere Friends. They will constantly bicker, leveraging the ongoing Army/USMC rivalry that my service friends and house mates have borne out over the years. They'll always be in one another's face until one of them is in a jam. Navy Girl will be the snotty, rich one. I guess that leaves Air Force Girl as the brainy one. I need to redesign her with some "Royal Smart Person" glasses.

Now for their foe.Their enemy will be something that conventional forces are powerless against.An evil scientist is trying to force her version of peace upon the world by activating a series of devices, whihc turn everything within their range of influence into the most adorable, chibified version of itself; Houses smiling with winking window eyes, anthropomorphic cars that bounce along on inner tube tires, dancing to songs sung by cherry trees with cherub cheeks. Never ending, sugar coated horror.All armies entering the Quarantine Transformation Zones, or QTZ's, are subject to these horrific, disfigurements, and only add to the strength of forces bent on building new devices and spreading the scourge of super-deformation globally.fav.me/d1q20xcBasically leveraging my love of darwing SD hardware.Only the Streamers can enter the QTZ, destroy the field generators, and free those trapped within. So once again, the worlds holds its breath, and waits for America to come save its ass.

In fact, it could even be possible that our widdle doctor, (Hmmm, Doctor Widdle? ) once cured of her rejuvenation, could revert back to her old self and work towards redemption by helping the Streamers against Shoujo Zero.Army Girl is afraid of heights, actually, but I really do need to give more thought to the various quirks of the other girls.
Plus, she stays cute.

Now for their foe.Their enemy will be something that conventional forces are powerless against.An evil scientist is trying to force her version of peace upon the world by activating a series of devices, whihc turn everything within their range of influence into the most adorable, chibified version of itself; Houses smiling with winking window eyes, anthropomorphic cars that bounce along on inner tube tires, dancing to songs sung by cherry trees with cherub cheeks. Never ending, sugar coated horror.All armies entering the Quarantine Transformation Zones, or QTZ's, are subject to these horrific, disfigurements, and only add to the strength of forces bent on building new devices and spreading the scourge of super-deformation globally.fav.me/d1q20xcBasically leveraging my love of darwing SD hardware.Only the Streamers can enter the QTZ, destroy the field generators, and free those trapped within. So once again, the worlds holds its breath, and waits for America to come save its ass.

Hah! Japan's answer to Captain America! This sounds like a menace for "season two". Of course, as far as anyone in the Streamers program knows, Japan produced no working Tactical Girls before turning their research over to the US. I'm sure the revelation of the existence of Shoujo-Zero would be quite an embarrassment for the JSDF. They may even call upon the American unit too late to save a major city of land mark from destruction; opting to attempt to contain her with Anti-Tactical Maid Weapons, developed in secret, as a countermeasure against the Streamers.The Streamers would be livid at the JSDF, right up until the moment they realize that the ATMW's actually came from the US.Starting as an adversary, the Streamers eventually realize that each of them could have been Shoujo-Zero under different circumstances. Of course this revelation will not changer the fact that SZ will have to be put down, for the greater good.Season Two would definitely be darker, featuring more intrigue, and higher character stakes than the QT War story arc.
BBEG?

Ah. I thought this was in reference to a specific anime, as was you mention of DBZ.I'm still brimming with ideas for this concept now. The opening should definitely involve the destruction of a Nimitz class carrier. (The fact that this happens so much in anime is what inspired Streamers to begin with.)A carrier group will be standing off shore in support of a special forces operation, launched to capture a rogue evil scientist. (I was going to call her Doctor Genius, but a quick google search revealed that name was taken. ) The troops dispatch the expected crowd of faceless hench dudes, and proceed to arrest the mad doctor, who is disquietingly okay with the whole affair.Soldier: "Come along quietly, doctor. We've taken care of your private little army. It's over!"Doctor: "Quiet the contrary, soldier. My army's just arrived."No sooner do the soldiers grab the doctor than her first Deformation Generator activates, turning everything, and everyone, on or around the island (Except for the doc herself, who as made herself immune to its effects with a special process that turned her skin a delightful shade of lavender.) into the most adorable, super deformed versions of themselves.Now with the might of history's most huggable carrier battle group, the maniacal doctor moves her plan of global conquest forward.Two additional thoughts.1.) One of the special forces soldiers initially confronting the dread doctor could be the future Special Forces Girl. Of course this raises the additional question of whether SFG is immune at first, of if she's liberated by the Streamers to later add to their ranks, with a thirst for vengeance created by her time as a super deformed thrall.2.) After watching Nichijou, I am wondering if I could get away with making the BBEG into a little girl, like Professor Hakase Shinonome from the anime, Nichijou? Would explain the obsession with cuteness.

These are some amazing suggestions!!! It hadn't occurred to me to have Special Forces Girl be covered up in any way, but that would give her that air of mystery like Snake Eyes from G.I. Joe or The Stig from Top Gear, or Raim Kawasaki from Bakuon, and I'm sure there are a multitude of examples I am not aware of. Having a long standing mystery regarding her appearance, before it is finally revealed that see is a survivor of the first deformation, sounds very satisfying, along with the big reveal that she hides herself because of the surreal beauty that is now her eternal curse.The idea of the chibified Aegis cruisers and Nimitz class carriers having the weakness of pool toy valves is inspired. In addition to just being plain cute and funny, it's a nice reference to the mandatory "weak point" that seems to afflict all genre monsters. And yes, the super deformation effect and the Streamer process, do share a similar technical origin. The evil doctor may well have been a high ranking scientist for the program, until her experiments with rejuvenation curse her with a skull too small for her mighty brain. In fact, it could even be possible that our widdle doctor, (Hmmm, Doctor Widdle? ) once cured of her rejuvenation, could revert back to her old self and work towards redemption by helping the Streamers against Shoujo Zero.Army Girl is afraid of heights, actually, but I really do need to give more thought to the various quirks of the other girls.

Never ending, sugar coated horror.All armies entering the Quarantine Transformation Zones, or QTZ's, are subject to these horrific, disfigurements, and only add to the strength of forces bent on building new devices and spreading the scourge of super-deformation globally.fav.me/d1q20xcBasically leveraging my love of darwing SD hardware.Only the Streamers can enter the QTZ, destroy the field generators, and free those trapped within. title: Skirted Swan
description: Even as rigidly censored as Science Ninja Team:Gatchaman (Gawd, who comes up with these titles?!) was in its Battle of the Planets incarnation, I distinctly remember more than one panty-shot of Princess (Jun the Swan to you purists. )  making through to my very American television screen.I must see the uncensored version one day. You know, to know the real story, and see the original artist's true intent all all that.
artist_notes: I saw that one. I like the part where she zips up her motorcycle leathers and you see a little fan-service jiggle.
I saw that one. I like the part where she zips up her motorcycle leathers and you see a little fan-service jiggle.

We saw it in the US back in 1978. It was marketed as Battle of the Planets to exploit the Star Wars craze. Since it was a full decade and a half before Power Rangers made over to these shores, it was unbelievable fresh and new to us. But they did edit out all the cool violence.Ironic, I was thinking of how back in the 1970's they repackage anime to appeal to Star Wars fans, while nowadays, the folks at Lucasfilm give Anakin Skywalker a spunky teenage sidekick to appeal to anime fans.Hmmm, I wonder exactly when Darth Vader murders Ahsoka Tano? He betrays and kills all the Jedi right? Or are the going to retcon that too.

Thanks for the kind words.It's funny. When I got on DA, and first received comments saying my work was reminiscent of what many identified as "old sküle" anime, I felt defensive and did a lot of research in post millennial animation. I was hoping to somehow "update" my style to be more palatable to a modern viewership. What I found however was that much of the new offerings from anime studios simply wasn't as appealing to me. Perhaps its a product of me being old and becoming "set in my ways", but I just felt that the character and mechanical designs of Japanese cartoons produced since the turn of the century have acquired a bland, flavorless sort of androgyny. Both character and machine alike look to be smoothed down and economized in detail and texture to optimize them for computer rendering. They all seem cold somehow. There's no life in them.The older works have a passion that communicates to me even now. There's something about knowing that every mecha in the most intricate battle scene are all hand drawn and cell painted that heightens my appreciation for the old forms. It's probably just the rantings of an "old sküle" fan, but I simply feel more passion from anime from the era you described.It is the greatest compliment you could possibly pay now to say I have taken this style and made it my own. I'm glad to know that such wonderful influences can be seen in my drawings. (I return the compliment to you as well, btw. You draw Lynn Minmei like nobody's business. ) I find it to be my privilege to spread this influence to a new generation. "Old Sküle" forever.

title: Uzi Cat Colored
description: Colors by  Phil Moyer. [link]
artist_notes: Yes, but not in the way you might think.The cat works for the mouse.  [link]

Well not as much fights as arguments. Constant, festering arguments.The cat harbors a belief that all felines naturally deserve to sun themselves in bookstore windows--a tradition dating back to ancient Egypt, and in so doing protect the premises from evil spirits and such. (In cat lore the accidental fire that immolated the fabled Library of Alexandria in 48BC occurred several hours after the attending librarian shooed the facilities guardian cat.)In exchange for this spiritual protection, librarians are afforded the additional privilege of caring for and feeding the cat that has deigned to safeguard their building with its anointed presence. Granted this was all in the ancient past when cats walked on four legs, and didn't talk, but it still mystifies Cassandra why Molly stubbornly insists upon her doing actual work. For Molly's part, it is a constant struggle to suppress her natural annoyance with cats in general, and Cassandra's religious devotion to laziness and rudeness in particular. The mouse frequently contemplates firing the feline, but every time the cat offends a customer with yet another round of "'Help YOU?' I'm a bookstore cat, YOU should be serving ME!" Cassandra turns around and saves the store from alien book thieves, or the firemen from Fahrenehit 451 intruding into reality.In fact there are times when the cat's inexplicable mastery of all known firearms and martial arts, makes the mouse momentarily consider the veracity of the "guardian cat" myth. Of course that still doesn't make Cassandra any more personable.

title: Long Legs of the Law
description: Well, not law exactly. More like Long Legs of Bounty Hunting, but that doesn't make for good alliteration.A viewer's comment reminded me that I had this drawing lying about.Rally Vincent is easily one of my favorite anime characters! She's like Pam Grier with big eyes.Seriously. Ever see the movie "Foxy Brown?" Let me tell you, Kenichi Sonoda must have!In the scene where Foxy draws her gun after distracting the female mob boss and her henchmen with a jar containing her husband/co-boss' family jewels preserved in formaldehyde*, Pam moves just like Rally Vincent as she mows down her foes. I wouldn't be surprised to learn they rotoscoped Ms. Grier to make the anime.* I know that was a bit of a spoiler, but com'on man, the film came out in 1974!
artist_notes: What can I say, Rally just brings out the naughty in me.
What can I say, Rally just brings out the naughty in me.

title: DMV: All Gangs Here
description: Ugh, This may well be the very first thing I ever colored on a computer, and it shows! The main reason I'm subjecting myself to inevitable torrent of disgusted gaffaws at my protean coloring attempt, is to display some previously unrevealed DMV characters.Long time viewers will recognize meter maid AJ and her lazy sidekick Drew.The white haired woman at the top left top is Roulette, hotshot officer of the GHP. (Global Highway Patrol.) A long time rival of AJ's, she's fond of pointing out that she has worldwide juridiction as well as a self-ware, nigh-indestructable squad car with a high-yield capacitor gun. Secretly, she's annoyed that AJ catches more crooks with just a gun that shoots parking tickets.On the right side of the composition, we witness the unfolding tradgedy of Pool Parties. Fierce battles by armed and armored car pools for territorial gain in the vast Megarage complex.In the silver minvan, we have the Hiocc's, (For High Occupancy vehicle.) who take advantage of the large carrying capacity of their vehicles, and use them as makeshift troop transports allowing them to overwhelm carpools driving smaller vehicles. Toward that end, I model all their SUV's after attack helicoptors. For instance, the silver minivan here is based on a Hughes 500 Littlebird.The green chopper carries two members of Motor Horde, one of the dying breed of biker gangs, who exist chiefly because, even at the time of this drawing I knew I would one day figure out how to do Akira style tail light trails in photoshop. The motorcycle is based on the old Green Machine toy. (If you don't know what that is, then your childhood sucked, and your parents didn't really love you.)Finally, going back to the top, the robot with the beret is a Bot-Nik. Bot-Nik's are fixtures at futuristic coffee houses. For the price of a single $10.00 coin (The not-too-distant-cyberpunk-dystopian-future's answer to the quarter.) they spontaneously generate free-verse poetry.They also serve as emergency thug muscle whenever the DMV or the GHP raid your coffee house. In the NTDCD future you see--coffee is illegal.
artist_notes: You're too kind.
You're too kind.

That's Lucille and Bo on the green thing, by the way.Now that I see the design again, I'm going to have to change it. This was back when I wanted a Recumbent Electricycle to look like a "grown-up" version of a Green Machine. (THE collest, baddest ass, most child-endangering evolution of the tricycle ever conceived. )Unfortunately the "Green Machine" design has no place for the chrome plated "faux-engine" noise maker.Back to the drawing board for me. I may just redraw this whole thing. So much has changed since I first did this.

Don't worry, I don't want to drop the whole Green Machine aesthetic entirely. But in the design above there's no place for an ostentatious mass of chrome piping, as described in my write up.Trust me, there is plenty of room for venal consumerism in the "NTDDCF".

I remember that show! I always loved the picture of a soaking wet Jane Badler that they'd show in the opening credits. Oh yea, and the pre-bunny, Energizer spokesman as the crazy sidekick.I didn't consciously bas the van on that truck. I actually based it on an MH-6 Little Bird helicopter. (I base all the commuter minivans in America Jone's world on attack helicpoters. Wait 'til you see the one I based on the Mil Mi-24. )But then the Highwayman's truck cab did transform into a helicopter, so perhaps it has influenced me afterall.

title: Visit Scenic Asgard
description: A two page spread I did for Mike Avone Oeming's Hammer of the Gods, of Heimdal the guard of the rainbow bridge of Asgard.That's a female frost giant looming in the background. It's been so long I've forgotten her significance to the story, although I do remember being disappointed I wasn't asked to draw any Valkyries.

title: Who Let the Dragon Out?
description: One of the advantages of being the sole proprietor of N.E. Boox (Otherwise known as The Most Dangerous Bookstore.) is the fact that, when giant fire-breathing serpents* start to terrorize the town, you know exactly where they came from.Someone's going to get a stern talking to when Molly gets back from lunch.* This particular breed of serpent is called a Bookwyrm. FYI.
artist_notes: Hmmm adults only? I'm going to have to let that idea percolate.
Hmmm adults only? I'm going to have to let that idea percolate.
Thank you for the kind words.
Thank you for the kind words.

title: BookStore Pencils
description: Pencils for a Most Dangerous Bookstore pin-up.Don't know why I gave Molly such an actiony pose, I like how it turned out although it seems a bit out of character to me. Yes, that's a shotgun behind Cassandra's back.

title: Wilma Deering
description: I desperately hope I'm not the only one who remembers the old Buck Rogers in the 25th Century television show.To me, it was just about the most awesome thing to be projected by a cathode ray tube! It had laser guns, hyper-tech futuristic cities, and there was no problem that couldn't be solved by an outer space dogfight!And best of all, there was Erin Gray in her painted-on flight suit!Televised gold.
artist_notes: Do you remember the episode where Gary Coleman was president?

Heh, I've been occasionally peeking at the new Buck Rogers comic book, and I am forced to wonder why they don't have the space Lugers, the star fighters, the wisecracking robots, the girls in tights, and the black president.* (The three-foot tall black president.)I wish I could get a job drawing for that publisher. I'd take care of that right away. I mean what's the point of Buck Rogers without Wilma's blue painted on flight suit?* It's funny. Back in 1978, having a black president was how you could tell the story took place in the far-flung future. Now that we actually have one, I guess we'll have to stick with the other tropes like food pils and personal space fighters.

Get the DVD of the first season. It's like James Bond (Roger Moore James Bond anyway.) in space.

The newer version does have that great Tom Jones song, which spoofs his earlier song, "Thunderball," and they add the Martian Queen to the cast , but you just can't beat the old 1950's cartoon short. [link]A little trivia; Originally published in 1928 by Philip Francis Nowlan, Buck Rogers actually predates even Flash Gordon.

It's from the 1970's.Even though Buck Rogers as a work of fiction originally predates even the venerable Flash Gordon, (I know, I know  "Flash!--Ah-Ah!"  ) it was resurrected in 1979 as part of the headlong rush to cash-in on the popularity of Star Wars.However, years before Lucas thought to untape Carrie Fisher's bosom and haul out the metal bikini, good ol' Glen Larson thought to play the "sex appeal" card with Erin Gray's wardrobe of "painted-on tight" jumpsuits.Good times.

Tee Hee. I think it was "Off Think." But whatever it was, the important thing was the result. I saw there was a new Buck Rogers comic book. I guess they were trying to be more like the classic incarnation, because from what I saw, there were no star fighters, no blue tights, and no alien princesses who wore bras as tops.And I'll bet they're even wondering why they aren't selling any copies.
It's probably just as well. The version you described here would probably end up banned somewhere.

Yea, BR was most awesome. And I did like it toward the end when Ardala and the Draconian forces joined forces with the EDD to fight off the evil forces of Julie Newmar.  She had a set of blue tights like Wilma's only her's had convenient openings to showcase her superior--er--hats. Recently, I saw that Dynamite Comics was doing a new Buck Rogers series, but it simply contained updated versions of 1930's designs. There was no reference to the Earth Defense Directorate, no James Bond style adventures, and unforgivably no Star Fighters. I really have to wonder just what the publisher was thinking.  Here they had a grand opportunity to capitalize on 70's/80's nostalgia and pubescent hormones, and they throw it all away to create a product that would only appeal to that handful of readers who were alive when Buster Crabbe was still making movies.Smart move Dynamite. I guess for now we'll have to rely upon our memories--and our DVD's.

title: Pool Girls
description: Another old commission I did for Phil Moyer. (I did a lot of drawings for my friend, all of which he is gladly making available to post. Many of them you will not see, as the intervening years have rendered them too shudder-worthy for me to admit to.)These are two OC's Ariel and Arisa (Sorry--Don'y who is which.) enjoying a swimming pool that happens to be atop a skyscaper somewhere in the not-too-distant-cyberpunk-future.

title: Galactica the Anime
description: Another commission for my buddy Phil Moyer.As I recall, this was of Kozue Kaori from Revolutionary Girl Utena dressed in colonial battle dress from Battlestar Galactica:TOS, superimposed over the image of the updated Galactica, flying in formation with a squadron of Mark II Vipers.That's a veritech-style Viper in battloid ("Robot" for you laymen! ) mode, pictured right behind Kozue.Fan fiction folks.  It's a long story.
artist_notes: Good thing to notice. Took me forever to realize that the differential in linewidth determined the relative depth of elements in a drawing.Embarrassingly enough, I had to have it pointed out to me.
Good thing to notice. Took me forever to realize that the differential in linewidth determined the relative depth of elements in a drawing.Embarrassingly enough, I had to have it pointed out to me.
I bow my head in shame that I didn't think to do that. Bug me latter if I do not rectify this glaring ommision in a future post.
I bow my head in shame that I didn't think to do that. Bug me latter if I do not rectify this glaring ommision in a future post.

Not an accident my friend. Trust me when I say everything in this picture is exactly as requested. And yes, the request for a Rebel Alliance patch on a Colonial battle jacket has me scratchin' my head too.* Now that you mention it, the choice of anime girl, also confuses me. If one was to choose from the cast of Revolutinary Girl, I'd figure Utena herself would make for a more appropriate Viper driver. However, as long as it's not salacious, (And sometimes even when it is.) it's all about what the Commssioner wants to see.* Phil does have a lengthy fan-fic that explains the conext of everything. [link] if you really want to know how everything fits together. Me? I'm just happy I found someone who wants to pay me to draw stuff.

The Cylons would naturally answer with a mecha Raider Imagine, a cross between a giant Centurian and a Raider. Giant robot gunfight.

I look at those three words and am taken aback by their inherent awesomeness. Actually, this reminds me, I am trying to decide what to put on my business cards:Mecha Master Like it says in the ad copy for the upcoming Empowered special?Mech Daddy My Armored Core® call sign? (Still the greatest video game every conceived by man. Whenever any poor fool tries to compare one of the woeful parade of inferior games that followed it to AC's beauteous majesty, I simply ask them, "Can you swap out the main character's legs for tank treads? If not, then shut the eff up!" )orGiant Robot Gunfight Because it's just cool to say.Decisions...decisions...

title: Tachiku?
description: Don't ask me why the Tachikoma (Took me FOREVER to figure out how to spell that!) has Pikachu nose art. This was a commission for my friend Phil Moyer.(Phil, do you have a DA username?)
artist_notes: Another Pokemon reference. It's Pikachu's finishing move. I thought it would be appropriate.
Another Pokemon reference. It's Pikachu's finishing move. I thought it would be appropriate.

Haw, that's my line pal. Curious thing is, you're the second person to ask me that this week.Really, I've only been published for color work. I've filled in the lines at Insight Studios, Image Comics, and IDW. (Pretty much every comic publisher whose name begins with an "I".)Beyond color work, not so much. A state of affairs I am presently working to change.Now you do work in the industry right? At least some branch of art and/or animation. I seem to remember us broaching the topic in an earlier correspondence.

Thank you very much.It seems many supremely talented souls from the animation field are looking too get into comics. I have to admit, I'm a little puzzled by that.Unless you work for a hallowed few companies, the pay is crap, and the jobs are scarce, and those doing the hiring are all aware there are legions of college students who'll do twice the work at half the price.I would think comics would be a step down from animation. Especially considering what you're bringing to the table. You must be a masochistic idiot like me who just loves comic books.

Well I'm wouldn't be one to stop you if you wanted to publish a comic book. I'm confident it would blow most of what's on the shelves--well--off the shelves.As for getting into animation. I actually do want to publish a comics this year, but beyond that, I honestly wouldn't know where to begin to approach the animation world.

Heh. I actually did once do my own animation.When I was a kid, I used to draw tiny animations in every large book in my parent's house! For while, during my childhood, every paperback novel and copy of Reader's Digest had a crude space battle, complete with the hero doing victory rolls as he flew triumphantly through the expanding debris cloud that was once his opponent, scrawled the upper right-hand corner of every page.I suppose the mere fact that I wasn't spanked within an inch of my life over this lengthy campaign of literary vandalism was my parents' own quiet way of encouraging my creativity. Anyway, that's the extremely long form way of saying I am passingly familiar with some of the principles of animation; although there are doubtless volumes of material I have yet to learn.What you propose here does sound interesting. To be honest, since comic book artists don't seem to have many of the advantages of any mailroom employee in any other type of company in the industrialized world, (Such as bi-weekly pay checks and heath benefits.) it actually does behoove me to investigate the other sources of income that can be acquired through my tendancy to scribble.
You've given me a lot to think about here.Thanks for the encouragement.

I have to admit, I'm a little puzzled by that.Unless you work for a hallowed few companies, the pay is crap, and the jobs are scarce, and those doing the hiring are all aware there are legions of college students who'll do twice the work at half the price.I would think comics would be a step down from animation. Especially considering what you're bringing to the table. You must be a masochistic idiot like me who just loves comic books.

Well I'm wouldn't be one to stop you if you wanted to publish a comic book. I'm confident it would blow most of what's on the shelves--well--off the shelves.As for getting into animation. I actually do want to publish a comics this year, but beyond that, I honestly wouldn't know where to begin to approach the animation world.

Heh. I actually did once do my own animation.When I was a kid, I used to draw tiny animations in every large book in my parent's house! For while, during my childhood, every paperback novel and copy of Reader's Digest had a crude space battle, complete with the hero doing victory rolls as he flew triumphantly through the expanding debris cloud that was once his opponent, scrawled the upper right-hand corner of every page.I suppose the mere fact that I wasn't spanked within an inch of my life over this lengthy campaign of literary vandalism was my parents' own quiet way of encouraging my creativity. Anyway, that's the extremely long form way of saying I am passingly familiar with some of the principles of animation; although there are doubtless volumes of material I have yet to learn.What you propose here does sound interesting. To be honest, since comic book artists don't seem to have many of the advantages of any mailroom employee in any other type of company in the industrialized world, (Such as bi-weekly pay checks and heath benefits.) it actually does behoove me to investigate the other sources of income that can be acquired through my tendancy to scribble.
You've given me a lot to think about here.Thanks for the encouragement.

title: I am Vengence, I am the Night
description: I AM...A cute little bat with barn-storming goggles.A commission I did of Barnaby Bat, an OC I created as a child, at the behest of my elementary school principle when he expressed concern that all my sketchbooks were full of war machines and explosions. (I hadn't yet discovered how to draw women at that point.) So while, the drawing is more or less recent the character predates all the other cute bat characters you've seen in lame Disney cartoons.Don't worry, I'll go back to babes and guns in my next submission.
artist_notes: Really, I'm more of a  fan.

title: El Hazard Collage
description: Another blast from the past in the prehistoric days before my discovery of Photoshop.I had it in my mind to draw all of the characters from the El Hazard ova. I tried to fit them all in there in this car wreck of a composition, but I still think I missed a couple.I drew this with Berol Prismacolor markers, and colored pencils. (Heh, by the time I was done, the marker fumes in my studio were so intense, a lit match probably would have blown up my entire house.)
artist_notes: Water walking comic book gawd, John Byrne always said, a cartoonist knows he's made it when a fan says, "I like your old stuff better."Sea-parting comic gawd, Adam Hughes says you know you made it when a fan says "I hate your effin' ghuts!!Of the two I'll take the Byrne senario.Thanks for the comment.

title: Biker Babe
description: This is a convention sketch I did some time ago. The commissioner was kind enough to send me a jpeg.In retrospect, I wish I had orieneted my paper in a landscape fashion, as opposed to the portrait orientation I went with here. It would've fit better, and allowed me to include more of the speeder bike. Even so it turned out surprising tolerable for something I threw together while trying to shill BGC posters. [link]And, yes, I'm fully aware that Princess Leia had long since changed out of her metal bikini by the time the speeder bike scene took place in Return of the Jedi, but a man can dream.

title: Gundam Girls 02
description: Natarle Badgiruel officer of the mobile suit carrier Archangel from Mobile Suit Gundam: Seed.I always like her character design, but I would pay good money to find out where the creators come up with these outlandish names.Actually, while we're on the subject of Gundam, does anyone know exactly what it is that distinguishes a Gundam above all other mobile suits? As far as I can tell Gundams are the only robots that have two eyes. (At least in the MSG universe.) I'm wondering if it's that what sets them apart.
artist_notes: That's the original MSG.(Actually, it was only called Pagasus in the novelization. In the anime, it called White Base and was as Pegasus-Class vessel.*) In MSG: Seed, the vessel was the Arcangel.*It is I, Dukkeofdeath, master of inconsequential factiods!!

Gundams are the hero mecha. They look like big blue samurai, and in general have two eyes, while all other mecha in the Gundam Universe have cycloptic single eyes.Gundams are also faster, more maneuverable, more durable; essentially superior in every measurable quality to any mech they're likely to meet in combat. But I guess I already said that when I said hero mecha.

I ink with Sakura Pigma Microns. I use the 08's for thick lines, and the 02's for the thin ones.One more thing, here's a trick an old college buddy taught me. When inking, place a blotter--a newspaper or a newsprint pad (with the cover removed. )--under your drawing. The cushioning will allow you to vary the pressure you put into the paper, and subsequently control the width of your line. This how you can lay down a single line that tapers at the end and is fat in the middle. You can also do this with a brush, or a pen nib, but this way is quicker and significantly less messy.For perspective, read this book. (Hell, sometimes I think I should work for Amazon.com. ) It shows clear concise tips to help anyone of any skill level raise their game in linear perspective.Doesn't matter if you draw manga-style or not. Regardless of how big your characters' eye are, Filippo Brunelleschi's rules style apply.

UC Gundam is where it's at. All the series in that continuity are so dense with a wonderful sense of historical verisimilitude. Very few animated series do as good a job as UCMSG at portray the march of technology and how if affects the course of human conflict.I'll always have a fond spot for the original MSG in how it depicted as new school of tactics emerging around mobile suit warfare. The newer series don't really have that feel. They're more like "In Sync In Space" to me.

title: Gundam Girls 01
description: Mobile Suit Gundam was a defining moment in anime. It was the first show to portray giant robots as mass produce piloted vehicles as opposed one-of-a-kind mechanical superheroes. It's influence is such that, it is often called the "Star Trek" of Japan.I suppose one of the secrets to the original MSG's success was the events surrounding the final fate of the mysterious Lala Sune. Her tragic end ignited an irreparable antipathy between the hero Amuro Ray, and the villain Char Aznable, (Man, these freaky names!)and set in motion circumstances that would lead to show's final act, as well motivate its sequels.But most importantly, without Lala, there was no ethereal glowing chick to turn the end of the story into stream-of-conciousness soup, like there is in 90% of Japanese animated features.She's pictured here with her Tricorn Hat mecha. You have no idea how hard I tried to make that goofy thing look menacing.
artist_notes: I thought I was giving it anime...style...shading  I've been listening to my marvel zombie buddies call my work anime-style for so long, frakly I was unprepared for the barrage of glowing praise for how American my work is. I'm definately missing something.If you could, point out some examples of what you would call anime-style shading. Clearly, I need to study more before I get this right.

You're talking about coloring eh? Well, I still get comments on how Marvelly, my colors are. The comments are all very positive which is cool, but I still would like to learn more from Japanese influence.

I wasn't evolution. It was revolution.When Marvel's six top artist's(Todd McFarlane, Jim Lee, Rob Liefeld, Marc Silvestri,Eric Larson, and Jim Valentino.)  jumped ship in 1992, and formed Image Comics, they introduced computer color into the mainstream.Their comics, drawn by the most saught after artists, and sporting obviously superior production values sold in the millions! Marvel and DC Comics were forced to dispense with their decades old printing techniques and completely retool in order to compete with a new demand for more lavish printing and paper. It's true most change the comic industry is downright glacial, but color was revolutionized practically overnight.

title: Ifurita
description: Back to basement mining. This is a very old picture I drew of Ifurita from the old "El Hazard" OVA. As this was before my descent into photoshop dependence, I drew this one in colored pencil.To the probable majority of you to whom El Hazard is "suma dat old school stuff," I recommend you give it a view. It's that rare anime that in spite of the fact that it has time travel, alternate dimensions, and world destroying, glowing goddesses, (Pictured above.) it still manages to have a satisfying conclusion that actually MAKES SENSE!Like I said--very rare indeed.
artist_notes: Thank you.
Thank you.
You do an underdrawing in brown for warm areas, and blue for cool areas.

More or less. Here, let me speak geek for a moment: Warm Colors are the colors of Sith light sabre blades--red, yellow, orange; Cool Colors are the color of Jedi light sabre blades-- green, blue, and violet. (That's right, Mace Windau's blade falls in the correct part of the spectrum.)Star Wars helped me through color theory. (Star Blazers helped me through astronomy. )

title: DMV: A New Breed
description: Oh, color this time.This was supposed to be the penultimate introduction strip, capturing that, "The Navy Calls it 'Fighter Weapons School'. The fliers call it..." sort of moment.I guess this would be for the Sunday paper.
artist_notes: Thanx, it took me a while before I was happy with the badge design. I always loved how elmblematic the badges were on the old Galaxy Rangers animated series, so I wanted to design something similarly symbollic, but comedic at the same time.

Yea, Rangers wrawct!I do wish Doc Hartford was even 1% more badass though. He was always bumbling around, and he had the lamest power.  He was the Ranger's equivalent of the heart kid from Captain Planet. Heck, it was his voice that sang the theme song, but in the Rock Concert episode they have Shane sing it! Doc got no love. Hmmmm. Drawing idea: Niko, in a girl's version of Shane's black "Galaxy Stranger" Uniform. Gotta remember that.

title: DMV: The Balding Blade
description: I'm sorry to have to post this strip so heinously out of sequence. I thought it was lost to the mists of time. Turns out it was just under the dust of the basement.  Happily, I found it while looking for something else. This was supposed to be the big moment where Blading Blade announces his famous cliche before claiming the "prize"--er--parking space.
artist_notes: Eh, nobody's perfect. That's doubly true for artists.
Eh, nobody's perfect. That's doubly true for artists.

title: Cold Weather Gear
description: After watching WAY too much Trigun I designed badassduster jackets and ticket rifles for my heroic meter maids.  Idon't know if it works to have them look so cool though. There's alittle more humor in having them fight road raging hordes in shortshorts.
artist_notes: I'll take your word for it. Whenever I see women standing on those windswept subway platforms in the dead of winter with skirts on, long or short, (Especially short. Brrrr!) I am overwhelmed with sympathy.I likes me some gams, but I can wait 'til the spring.
Those are actually warm? I find that hard to believe.

Hmm that reminds me. Have you noticed all the good westerns are coming from anywhere but the US? Cartoon Westerns are done by the Japanese (Case in point, the aforementioned Trigun), Western comic books are done by the Europeans. Even Modern Western movies like 3:10 to Yuma star English and Australian actors. I really find that odd.Well the was that excellent sci-fi western Firefly, but they were in all kinds of a hurry to cancel that.
They're making a movie? Please tell me its an animated movie and not some horrifying Hollywood cash in. (I still wake up screaming at the thought of DiCaprio's Akira.)
I'm glad to hear that the Trigun is animated.I wish I was joking about the Akira thing though. [link]  I just hope it gets lost in "development hell."
Because they like the sound we make when we scream.

Well then they probably just like yo watch you squirm. (If you bear any resemblance to your avatar, I wouldn't mind seeing that myself.   But I digress.)Sadly, it will only end when they stop making money. Ironically, that'll be about the time they start actually making good movies.

Hah, you weren't kidding when you said you loved Vash.Curious thing, I was flirting with the idea of making AJ's partner female. (Since I'm not yet published, I can change the cannon at will. I know it doesn't necessarily mean I should, but I can. ) Anyway, these pictures are a strong argument in favor of a female Drew.
So no wardrobe malfunction. Boy, you really went all out for this costume. I was already impressed by the jacket.
I dunno. "A god among freaks" sound pretty fun.And instead of half-assing it, you're half-chesting it. (Pun -1,000 HP)Okay, I'll stop.

title: DMV: There Can Be Only Wha?
description: In the heroic nick of time, the Balding Blade is prevented from beheading  Shades Wearing Business Suit Guy by having his Dragon's Head Katana plucked from his hand by an expertly launched adhesive parking ticket.Who could be behind this?Aw come on, the movie opened in a parking garage! You really think I was going to let that lie?
artist_notes: The pistol is called a Dispenser. I wanted an ironic, innocuous sounding name. (Although, oddly you're by no means the first person to suggest "Violator.")
The pistol is called a Dispenser. I wanted an ironic, innocuous sounding name. (Although, oddly you're by no means the first person to suggest "Violator.")
I was always considering a clip full of tightly folded tickets that are printed upon as the exit the muzzle, but your suggestion of an ink cartridge is downright inspiring.May have to reconsider.

title: DMV: Dueling Swords
description: Flash forward ( Past all those years of pesky charcaterdevelopemnt.) to the present.The most valuable thing in the DMVnot-too-distant-cyberpunk-dystopian-future is a 6 x 3 meterrectangle of road surface watched over by a cycloptic parking meterand delineated by two inch wide lines of paint.  These simple sliversof territory are easily worth a man's life --especially during rushhour! When the spaces become scarce, ritualized combat begins.
artist_notes: The thing is, you can earn your share or food water and natural resources--you just have to show up on time for work. Thus the demand for parking space.And believe me, the combat is going to get a lot uglier.I'll tell you about old ladies later.

The thing is, you can earn your share or food water and natural resources--you just have to show up on time for work. Thus the demand for parking space.And believe me, the combat is going to get a lot uglier.I'll tell you about old ladies later.

title: DMV: Whale Tale
description: Young AJ  notices the brake lights aren't even lit on theclassic IROCZ Camaro, as it peels away from nearly crushing theyoung girl beneath its wheels.  She memorizes its distinctiveaftermarket "Whale Tail" spoiler, (Bet you were wonderingwhat the title of this post had to do with the strip. ) torecall later in the inevitable rematch, and looks in speechless awe uponher positive black role model.A meter maid is born.
artist_notes: I was talking to a friend earlier this week about military jets, and that song came in my head when I thought the F-15 Eagle. Since its engines are so ungodly huge for a fighter plane, they  had to build these big ol' bumps into its rear fuslage. Coupled with the subtle hour glass shape of its area rule supersonic airframe, the Eagle always reminded me of a woman with a big ol' padonkabomp rump. [link] So you're not the only one. Of course that could just mean we both need therapy.

Every one loves Camaros. You don't own a Camaro, a Camaro owns you.A mistic bond exists between a Camaro and it's owner quite unlike the rapport between any other car and driver. It's appropriate that Spike in the Transfomers movie befriends a Camaro. I know Bumblebee is actually a Volkswagon Beetle, but in the context if the movie the change was acceptable.  (And that "Bring the Rain" A-10 sequence absolootly wrawct!)

title: DMV: The Rescue
description: Young AJ is deftly plucked from the front bumper of death by aquick thinking meter maid. (Technical Note: To help ground this ina previous era, I decided to give the older meter maid, actual wheelson her scooter.)I think you see where this is all going.
artist_notes: Still appreciating the love.
Still appreciating the love.

title: DMV: Killer Camaero
description: Last week a curious poster asked if I could show him some actualnarrative art to some of these high concepts I've been soliberally hurling about.  Thanks to my celebrated bachelor's inabilityto throw away anything, I am able to oblige.While I do have pages in the traditional comic book format, Ithought it would be fun to start out with this origin story for thecharacter AJ.  Be advised, this is back when I was crazy enoughto try this out as a comic strip This first strip shows a seminal moment in AJ's childhood, whenshe wandered into the mighty Megarage and was menaced by anantique Camaro IROCZ. ( Keen eyes will notice the mass ofunpaid tickets accumulated on the car's windshield.) Thosedecrying child endangerment please calm down. As you can see,AJ's deliverance is at hand.
artist_notes: It would be great if it did. I can tell you with absolute certainty, newspaper comic syndicates have no interest in action/adventure comics. The last new action comic that was of any worth was Gil Kane's Star Hawks and that was printed in 1979!If you want a comic strip with action. (Or original thought, or intelligent humor for that matter.) you have to look online.Glad you liked the scooter.

Yea the only action strips are holdovers from the golden age of comics like Dick Tracy, and Brenda Starr: Reporter. But even those are watered far down from their former glory.Have you ever read early Dick Tracy? In the first week, crooks kidnap his fiance', shoot her father in the chest, steal his life savings, and sucker punch Dick with a billy club when he tries to intercede. (This all happens on the page, no chickenshit cutting away or hinting at violence!) When Dick comes to, he surveys the wreckage of his life and swears a bitter oath on his fiance's father's corps that he will become the scourge of crime. (Yea, I know, it sounds all Batmanny.  Hey, it was the 1930's. A lot of people were swearing vengeful oaths against crime back then. Every other alley was full of kneeling dudes, cradling a recently deceased loved one in one hand and raising their free hand toward the heavens begging the almighty for the power to lay their enemies low. You couldn't sleep with all the oaths being belted out.)Anyway, if someone approached a modern comic syndicate with Dick Tracy's exact premise and events, he'd be shown the door. Newspaper comics are in a sad state, in my opinion.
Probably right.

You couldn't sleep with all the oaths being belted out.)Anyway, if someone approached a modern comic syndicate with Dick Tracy's exact premise and events, he'd be shown the door. Newspaper comics are in a sad state, in my opinion.
Probably right.
Probably right.
Probably right.

Yup, of course I've since decided to change the car from the Camaro to the fictional Red ICE, (Still working on the name, but I want the letters ICE in it to signify Internal Combustion Engine. )That way, I avoid any potential licensing issues with General Motors, and I get to have fun making up another car.

I'd be legitimately surprised to meet anyone who didn't have at least a passing familiarity with the bold concept cars of that era. Cars such as the infamous Lincoln Futura. (And if you can't tell me why that particular car is famous, you have to turn in your fan girl membership card.)I have one minor point to make. Forgive me, it pains me to respond your endearing conversational overture with an anal-retentive correction, but I'm afraid that nerd OCD has taken control of my typing hand. "Retro-Future" is a term used by modern designers to describe designs that are inspired by the aforementioned concept vehicles. Cars of the 1950's, many of with were inspired by jet aircraft, were described at the time as simply being "futuristic."Retro-futurism harks back to past generations' visions of the future.But anyway, yes I am familiar with Art-Deco, Jet-Age and Modernist design.

title: The Two Marks
description: Colonial Viper Mark I from classic Battlestar Galactica, and ViperMark II from the new series. The Real Stars of either show.To me anyway.
artist_notes: January 16th. I know I'm amped.
January 16th. I know I'm amped.
Trust me, I have no problem whatsoever with faves.
Trust me, I have no problem whatsoever with faves.

Yea, I wish they used the Mark One's more in the new show than just having one in a museum. As I recall, the 'Two's were all museum specimens as well. The cataclysm should have required the use of all available resources.BTW, what do you think of the rumors of Bryan Singer directing an all new update of the classic show? [link]

title: Hench Bunny
description: This is a character from a comic strip I drew waay back in mycollege days. (I actually had a professor request a sketch of herat the end of a term. )She didn't normally dress as a Playboy bunny, but for some reason thelook works on her.
artist_notes: To each his own.
To each his own.
Naw, the original out fit was a latex halter top and micro mini skirt combo, complete with stiletto pumps. (Much more conservative than what you see here. )

title: Golden Boyfriend
description: Pictured above are evil prom queen Lori, and OttØ, her motorcycle riding bad boy boyfriend bot. (Alliteration x4 combo! ) As you can tell, this is one of many variations1 I experimented with our resident mechanical bully. I based this version's face loosely on Gort from "The Day the Earth Stood Still.2"You'll note that the top of his brain case has a paisley pattern. This is to simulate the bandanna that all worthless teenage reprobates are required to wear 3. The rest of his chasis is electroplated with gold as the ultimate expression of "bling.4" I never quite figured out OttØ's backstory. Is he a high-end military prototype, expertly programmed to piss off the fathers of rich young women and steal the lunch money from weak young men.? Is he teenage boy, disfigured in a horrible accident? Is he teenage boy who had his brain swapped into a killer robot chasis beacuse he thought it would be a "bitchin' thing to do"? Or is he simply a mass produced personal computer/cell phone, like out of Chobits ? No one possibility has stuck out to me as the most fun, so for now I simply work from the fact he's a menacing mechanical miscreant (Moar Alliteration!  Gimme pointz!) who is inexplicably enrolled in a private high school5.  Also, he has gyroscopic equilibrium, so he can strut in perfectly simulated slow motion while blasting badass music on his internal sound system.  Oh, and lastly, he never leaves Lori's side6. Loriand OttØ expend much energy in cynical attempts to prove how little they care about each other. (Protesting too strongly, as is the cliche'. ) As a couple, they're very much like The Baroness and Destro; only--you know-- in high school. SELF-INDULGENT FOOTNOTES1.) I'm guessing this would be "OttØ 5.0. 2.) Not the Keanu Reeves version!3.) It's part of the bad boy code--look it up. 4.) Something he thought was cool, until the ever maligning Lori started calling him "C3P0"! 5. Yea, yea I saw Cromartie High , but I'm hoping girls will find OttØ sexier than Mechazawa. 6 Well, mostly never.  there are alot of other pretty girls around, and like an FA-18, OttØ can track 24 targets at once!
artist_notes: It's from a defunct old project set in a futuristic high school, ruled absolutely by an evil shrew and her minions.The various characters are spread about my gallery. I invite you to peruse at your convenience and comment, should you feel so inclined.
Glad you did. I've been enjoying your comments.
